parts.title: 20. Purification by knowledge & vision of what is/is not the path

rows.pali: 692.Ayaṃ maggo, ayaṃ na maggoti evaṃ maggañca amaggañca ñatvā ṭhitaṃ ñāṇaṃ pana maggāmaggañāṇadassanavisuddhi nāma.
rows.english: 1.The knowledge established by getting to know the path and the not- path thus, “This is the path, this is not the path,” is called “purification by knowledge and vision of what is the path and what is not the path.”

rows.pali: Taṃ sampādetukāmena kalāpasammasanasaṅkhātāya nayavipassanāya tāva yogo karaṇīyo.
rows.english: 2.One who desires to accomplish this should first of all apply himself to the inductive insight called “comprehension by groups.1”

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why?

rows.pali: Āraddhavipassakassa obhāsādisambhave maggāmaggañāṇasambhavato.
rows.english: Because knowledge of what is the path and what is not the path appears in connection with the appearance of illumination, etc. (XX.105f.) in one who has begun insight.

rows.pali: Āraddhavipassakassa hi obhāsādīsu sambhūtesu maggāmaggañāṇaṃ hoti, vipassanāya ca kalāpasammasanaṃ ādi.
rows.english: For it is after illumination, etc., have appeared in one who has already begun insight that there comes to be knowledge of what is the path and what is not the path. And comprehension by groups is the beginning of insight.

rows.pali: Tasmā etaṃ kaṅkhāvitaraṇānantaraṃ uddiṭṭhaṃ.
rows.english: That is why it is set forth next to the overcoming of doubt.

rows.pali: Apica yasmā tīraṇapariññāya vattamānāya maggāmaggañāṇaṃ uppajjati, tīraṇapariññā ca ñātapariññānantarā, tasmāpi taṃ maggāmaggañāṇadassanavisuddhiṃ sampādetukāmena kalāpasammasane tāva yogo kātabbo.
rows.english: Besides, knowledge of what is the path and what is not the path arises when “full-understanding as investigation” is occurring, and full-understanding as investigation comes next to full- understanding as the known (see XIX.21). So this is also a reason why one who desires to accomplish this purification by knowledge and vision of what is the path and what is not the path should first of all apply himself to comprehension by groups.

rows.pali: 693.Tatrāyaṃ vinicchayo – tisso hi lokiyapariññā ñātapariññā tīraṇapariññā pahānapariññā ca.
rows.english: 3.Here is the exposition: there are three kinds of mundane full-understanding, that is, full-understanding as the known, full-understanding as investigation, and full-understanding as abandoning,

rows.pali: Yā sandhāya vuttaṃ "abhiññāpaññā ñātaṭṭhe ñāṇaṃ.
rows.english: with reference to which it was said: “Understanding that is direct-knowledge is knowledge in the sense of being known.

rows.pali: Pariññāpaññā tīraṇaṭṭhe ñāṇaṃ.
rows.english: Understanding that is full-understanding is knowledge in the sense of investigating.

rows.pali: Pahānapaññā pariccāgaṭṭhe ñāṇa"nti (paṭi. ma. 1.75).
rows.english: Understanding that is abandoning is knowledge in the sense of giving up” (Paṭis I 87).

rows.pali: Tattha "ruppanalakkhaṇaṃ rūpaṃ, vedayitalakkhaṇā vedanā"ti evaṃ tesaṃ tesaṃ dhammānaṃ paccattalakkhaṇasallakkhaṇavasena pavattā paññā ñātapariññā nāma.
rows.english: Herein, the understanding that occurs by observing the specific characteristics of such and such states thus, “Materiality (rūpa) has the characteristic of being molested (ruppana); feeling has the characteristic of being felt,” is called full- understanding as the known.

rows.pali: "Rūpaṃ aniccaṃ, vedanā aniccā"tiādinā nayena tesaṃyeva dhammānaṃ sāmaññalakkhaṇaṃ āropetvā pavattā lakkhaṇārammaṇikavipassanā paññā tīraṇapariññā nāma.
rows.english: The understanding consisting in insight with the general characteristics as its object that occurs in attributing a general characteristic to those same states in the way beginning, “Materiality is impermanent, feeling is impermanent” is called full-understanding as investigation.2

rows.pali: Tesuyeva pana dhammesu niccasaññādipajahanavasena pavattā lakkhaṇārammaṇikavipassanā paññā pahānapariññā nāma.
rows.english: The understanding consisting in insight with the characteristics as its object that occurs as the abandoning of the perception of permanence, etc., in those same states is called full-understanding as abandoning.

rows.pali: Tattha saṅkhāraparicchedato paṭṭhāya yāva paccayapariggahā ñātapariññāya bhūmi.
rows.english: 4.Herein, the plane of full-understanding as the known extends from the delimitation of formations (Ch. XVIII) up to the discernment of conditions (Ch. XIX);

rows.pali: Etasmiṃ hi antare dhammānaṃ paccattalakkhaṇapaṭivedhasseva ādhipaccaṃ hoti.
rows.english: for in this interval the penetration of the specific characteristics of states predominates.

rows.pali: Kalāpasammasanato pana paṭṭhāya yāva udayabbayānupassanā tīraṇapariññāya bhūmi.
rows.english: The plane of full-understanding as investigation extends from comprehension by groups up to contemplation of rise and fall (XXI.3f.);

rows.pali: Etasmiṃ hi antare sāmaññalakkhaṇapaṭivedhasseva ādhipaccaṃ hoti.
rows.english: for in this interval the penetration of the general characteristics predominates.

rows.pali: Bhaṅgānupassanaṃ ādiṃ katvā upari pahānapariññāya bhūmi.
rows.english: The plane of full-understanding as abandoning extends from contemplation of dissolution onwards (XXI.10);

rows.pali: Tato paṭṭhāya hi aniccato anupassanto niccasaññaṃ pajahati, dukkhato anupassanto sukhasaññaṃ, anattato anupassanto attasaññaṃ, nibbindanto nandiṃ, virajjanto rāgaṃ, nirodhento samudayaṃ, paṭinissajjanto ādānaṃ pajahatīti (paṭi. ma. 1.52) evaṃ niccasaññādipahānasādhikānaṃ sattannaṃ anupassanānaṃ ādhipaccaṃ.
rows.english: for from there onwards the seven contemplations that effect the abandoning of the perception of permanence, etc., predominate thus: (1) Contemplating [formations] as impermanent, a man abandons the perception of permanence. (2) Contemplating [them] as painful, he abandons the perception of pleasure. (3) Contemplating [them] as not-self, he abandons the perception of self. (4) Becoming dispassionate, he abandons delighting. (5) Causing fading away, he abandons greed. (6) Causing cessation, he abandons originating. (7) Relinquishing, he abandons grasping (Paṭis I 58).3

rows.pali: Iti imāsu tīsu pariññāsu saṅkhāraparicchedassa ceva paccayapariggahassa ca sādhitattā iminā yoginā ñātapariññāva adhigatā hoti, itarā ca adhigantabbā.
rows.english: 5. So, of these three kinds of full-understanding, only full-understanding as the known has been attained by this meditator as yet, which is because the delimitation of formations and the discernment of conditions have already been accomplished; the other two still remain to be attained.

rows.pali: Tena vuttaṃ "yasmā tīraṇapariññāya vattamānāya maggāmaggañāṇaṃ uppajjati, tīraṇapariññā ca ñātapariññānantarā, tasmāpi taṃ maggāmaggañāṇadassanavisuddhiṃ sampādetukāmena kalāpasammasane tāva yogo kātabbo"ti.
rows.english: Hence it was said above: “Besides, knowledge of what is the path and what is not the path arises when full-understanding as investigation is occurring, and full-understanding as investigation comes next to full-understanding as the known. So this is also a reason why one who desires to accomplish this purification by knowledge and vision of what is the path and what is not the path should first of all apply himself to comprehension by groups” (§2).

rows.pali: 694.Tatrāyaṃ pāḷi –
rows.english: 6.Here is the text:

rows.pali: Ettha ca cakkhuṃ - pe - jarāmaraṇanti iminā peyyālena dvārārammaṇehi saddhiṃ dvārappavattā dhammā, pañcakkhandhā, cha dvārāni, cha ārammaṇāni, cha viññāṇāni, cha phassā, cha vedanā, cha saññā, cha cetanā, cha taṇhā, cha vitakkā, cha vicārā, cha dhātuyo, dasa kasiṇāni, dvattiṃsakoṭṭhāsā, dvādasāyatanāni, aṭṭhārasa dhātuyo, bāvīsati indriyāni, tisso dhātuyo, nava bhavā, cattāri jhānāni, catasso appamaññā, catasso samāpattiyo, dvādasa paṭiccasamuppādaṅgānīti ime dhammarāsayo saṃkhittāti veditabbā.
rows.english: 9.Herein, the abbreviation, “The eye … (etc.) … Ageing-and-death,” should be understood to represent the following sets of things elided: 1. The states that occur in the doors [of consciousness] together with the doors and the objects. 2. The five aggregates. 3. The six doors. 4. The six objects. 5. The six kinds of consciousness. 6. The six kinds of contact. 7. The six kinds of feeling. 8. The six kinds of perception. 9. The six kinds of volition. 10. The six kinds of craving. 11. The six kinds of applied thought. 12. The six kinds of sustained thought. 13. The six elements. 14. The ten kasiṇas. 15. The thirty-two bodily aspects. 16. The twelve bases. 17. The eighteen elements. 18. The twenty-two faculties. 19. The three elements. 20. The nine kinds of becoming. 21. The four jhānas. 22. The four measureless states. 23. The four [immaterial] attainments. 24. The twelve members of the dependent origination.

rows.pali: Vuttaṃ hetaṃ abhiññeyyaniddese –
rows.english: 10. For this is said in the Paṭisambhidā in the description of what is to be directly known:

rows.pali: Taṃ tattha evaṃ vitthārena vuttattā idha sabbaṃ peyyālena saṃkhittaṃ.
rows.english: 12.Since all this detail is given there it has been abbreviated here.

rows.pali: Evaṃ saṃkhitte panettha ye lokuttarā dhammā āgatā, te asammasanupagattā imasmiṃ adhikāre na gahetabbā.
rows.english: But what is thus abbreviated includes the supramundane states. These should not be dealt with at this stage because they are not amenable to comprehension.

rows.pali: Yepi ca sammasanupagā, tesu ye yassa pākaṭā honti sukhena pariggahaṃ gacchanti, tesu tena sammasanaṃ ārabhitabbaṃ.
rows.english: And as regards those that are amenable to comprehension a beginning should be made by comprehending those among them that are obvious to and easily discernible by the individual [meditator].

rows.pali: 695.Tatrāyaṃ khandhavasena ārabbhavidhānayojanā – yaṃkiñci rūpaṃ - pe - sabbaṃ rūpaṃ aniccato vavatthapeti, ekaṃ sammasanaṃ.
rows.english: 13.Here is the application of the directions dealing with the aggregates: “Any materiality whatever, (i–iii) whether past, future, or present, (iv–v) internal or external, (vi–vii) gross or subtle, (viii–ix) inferior or superior, (x-xi) far or near— he defines all materiality as impermanent: this is one kind of comprehension.

rows.pali: Dukkhato anattato vavatthapeti, ekaṃ sammasananti.
rows.english: He defines it as painful: this is one kind of comprehension. He defines it as not-self: this is one kind of comprehension” (see §6).

rows.pali: Ettāvatā ayaṃ bhikkhu "yaṃkiñci rūpa"nti evaṃ aniyamaniddiṭṭhaṃ sabbampi rūpaṃ atītattikena ceva catūhi ca ajjhattādidukehīti ekādasahi okāsehi paricchinditvā sabbaṃ rūpaṃ aniccato vavatthapeti, aniccanti sammasati.
rows.english: At this point this bhikkhu [takes] all materiality, which is described without specifying as “any materiality whatever,” and having delimited it in the eleven instances, namely, with the past triad and with the four dyads beginning with the internal dyad, he “defines all materiality as impermanent,” he comprehends that it is impermanent.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ ?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: Parato vuttanayena.
rows.english: In the way stated next.

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ – "rūpaṃ atītānāgatapaccuppannaṃ aniccaṃ khayaṭṭhenā"ti (paṭi. ma. 1.48).
rows.english: For this is said: “Materiality, whether past, future or present, is impermanent in the sense of destruction.”

rows.pali: Tasmā esa yaṃ atītaṃ rūpaṃ, taṃ yasmā atīteyeva khīṇaṃ, nayimaṃ bhavaṃ sampattanti aniccaṃ khayaṭṭhena.
rows.english: 14. Accordingly, he comprehends the materiality that is past as “impermanent in the sense of destruction” because it was destroyed in the past and did not reach this becoming;

rows.pali: Yaṃ anāgataṃ anantarabhave nibbattissati, tampi tattheva khīyissati, na tato paraṃ bhavaṃ gamissatīti aniccaṃ khayaṭṭhena.
rows.english: and he comprehends the materiality that is future as “impermanent in the sense of destruction” since it will be produced in the next becoming, will be destroyed there too, and will not pass on to a further becoming;

rows.pali: Yaṃ paccuppannaṃ rūpaṃ, tampi idheva khīyati, na ito gacchatīti aniccaṃ khayaṭṭhena.
rows.english: and he comprehends the materiality that is present as “impermanent in the sense of destruction” since it is destroyed here and does not pass beyond.

rows.pali: Yaṃ ajjhattaṃ, tampi ajjhattameva khīyati, na bahiddhābhāvaṃ gacchatīti aniccaṃ khayaṭṭhena.
rows.english: And he comprehends the materiality that is internal as “impermanent in the sense of destruction” since it is destroyed as internal and does not pass on to the external state.

rows.pali: Yaṃ bahiddhā - pe - oḷārikaṃ - pe - sukhumaṃ - pe - hīnaṃ - pe - paṇītaṃ - pe - dūre - pe - santike, tampi tattheva khīyati, na dūrabhāvaṃ gacchatīti aniccaṃ khayaṭṭhenāti sammasati.
rows.english: And he comprehends the materiality that is external … gross … subtle … inferior … superior … far … And he comprehends the materiality that is near as “impermanent in the sense of destruction” since it is destroyed there and does not pass on to the far state. And all this is impermanent in the sense of destruction.

rows.pali: Idaṃ sabbampi "aniccaṃ khayaṭṭhenā"ti etassa vasena ekaṃ sammasanaṃ.
rows.english: Accordingly, there is “one kind of comprehension” in this way;

rows.pali: Bhedato pana ekādasavidhaṃ hoti.
rows.english: but it is effected in eleven ways.

rows.pali: Sabbameva ca taṃ dukkhaṃ bhayaṭṭhena.
rows.english: 15.And all that [materiality] is “painful in the sense of terror.”

rows.pali: Bhayaṭṭhenāti sappaṭibhayatāya.
rows.english: In the sense of terror because of its terrifyingness;

rows.pali: Yañhi aniccaṃ, taṃ bhayāvahaṃ hoti sīhopamasutte (saṃ. ni. 3.78; a. ni. 4.33) devānaṃ viya.
rows.english: for what is impermanent brings terror, as it does to the deities in the Sīhopama Sutta (S III 84).

rows.pali: Iti idampi "dukkhaṃ bhayaṭṭhenā"ti etassa vasena ekaṃ sammasanaṃ.
rows.english: So this is also painful in the sense of terror. Accordingly, there is one kind of comprehension in this way too;

rows.pali: Bhedato pana ekādasavidhaṃ hoti.
rows.english: but it is effected in eleven ways.

rows.pali: Yathā ca dukkhaṃ, evaṃ sabbampi taṃ anattā asārakaṭṭhena.
rows.english: 16.And just as it is painful, so too all that [materiality] is “not-self in the sense of having no core.”

rows.pali: Asārakaṭṭhenāti "attā nivāsī kārako vedako sayaṃvasī"ti evaṃ parikappitassa attasārassa abhāvena.
rows.english: In the sense of having no core because of the absence of any core of self conceived as a self, an abider, a doer, an experiencer, one who is his own master;

rows.pali: Yañhi aniccaṃ, dukkhaṃ, taṃ attanopi aniccataṃ vā udayabbayapīḷanaṃ vā vāretuṃ na sakkoti, kuto tassa kārakādibhāvo.
rows.english: for what is impermanent is painful (S III 82), and it is impossible to escape the impermanence, or the rise and fall and oppression, of self, so how could it have the state of a doer, and so on?

rows.pali: Tenāha – "rūpañca hidaṃ, bhikkhave, attā abhavissa.
rows.english: Hence it is said, “Bhikkhus, were materiality self,

rows.pali: Nayidaṃ rūpaṃ ābādhāya saṃvatteyyā"tiādi (saṃ. ni. 3.59).
rows.english: it would not lead to affliction” (S III 66), and so on.

rows.pali: Iti idampi "anattā asārakaṭṭhenā"ti etassa vasena ekaṃ sammasanaṃ.
rows.english: So this is also not-self in the sense of having no core. Accordingly, there is one kind of comprehension in this way too,

rows.pali: Bhedato pana ekādasavidhaṃ hoti.
rows.english: but it is effected in eleven ways.

rows.pali: Esa nayo vedanādīsu.
rows.english: The same method applies to feeling, and so on.

rows.pali: 696.Yaṃ pana aniccaṃ, taṃ yasmā niyamato saṅkhatādibhedaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 17.But what is impermanent is necessarily classed as formed, etc., and so in order to show the synonyms for that [impermanence],

rows.pali: Tenassa pariyāyadassanatthaṃ, nānākārehi vā manasikārappavattidassanatthaṃ "rūpaṃ atītānāgatapaccuppannaṃ aniccaṃ saṅkhataṃ paṭiccasamuppannaṃ khayadhammaṃ vayadhammaṃ virāgadhammaṃ nirodhadhamma"nti puna pāḷi vuttā.
rows.english: or in order to show how the attention given to it occurs in different ways, it is restated in the text thus: “Materiality, whether past, future, or present, is impermanent, formed, dependently arisen, subject to destruction, subject to fall, subject to fading away, subject to cessation” (§7).

rows.pali: Esa nayo vedanādīsūti.
rows.english: The same method applies to feeling, and so on.

rows.pali: 697.So tasseva pañcasu khandhesu aniccadukkhānattasammasanassa thirabhāvatthāya, yaṃ taṃ bhagavatā "katamehi cattārīsāya ākārehi anulomikaṃ khantiṃ paṭilabhati, katamehi cattārīsāya ākārehi sammattaniyāmaṃ okkamatī"ti etassa vibhaṅge –
rows.english: 18. Now, when the Blessed One was expounding conformity knowledge, he [asked the question]: “By means of what forty aspects does he acquire liking that is in conformity? By means of what forty aspects does he enter into the certainty of rightness? ” (Paṭis II 238). 4 In the answer to it comprehension of impermanence, etc., is set forth by him analytically in the way beginning:

rows.pali: Cattārīsāya ākārehi,
"Pañcakkhandhe aniccato passanto anulomikaṃ khantiṃ paṭilabhati.
rows.english: Seeing the five aggregates as impermanent, he acquires liking that is in conformity.

rows.pali: Pañcannaṃ khandhānaṃ nirodho niccaṃ nibbānanti passanto sammattaniyāmaṃ okkamatī"tiādinā (paṭi. ma. 3.38) nayena,
rows.english: And seeing that the cessation of the five aggregates is the permanent Nibbāna, he enters into the certainty of rightness” (Paṭis II 238).

rows.pali: Anulomañāṇaṃ vibhajantena pabhedato aniccādisammasanaṃ vuttaṃ.
rows.english: So in order to strengthen that same comprehension of impermanence, pain, and not-self in the five aggregates,

rows.pali: Tassāpi vasena ime pañcakkhandhe sammasati.
rows.english: this [meditator] also comprehends these five aggregates by means of that [kind of comprehension].

rows.pali: 698.Kathaṃ ?
rows.english: 19.How does he do it?

rows.pali: So hi ekekaṃ khandhaṃ anaccantikatāya, ādiantavantatāya ca aniccato.
rows.english: He does it by means of comprehension as impermanent, etc., stated specifically as follows: He comprehends each aggregate as impermanent because of non-endlessness, and because of possession of a beginning and an end;

rows.pali: Uppādavayapaṭipīḷanatāya, dukkhavatthutāya ca dukkhato.
rows.english: as painful because of oppression by rise and fall, and because of being the basis for pain;

rows.pali: Paccayayāpanīyatāya, rogamūlatāya ca rogato.
rows.english: as a disease because of having to be maintained by conditions, and because of being the root of disease;

rows.pali: Dukkhatāsūlayogitāya, kilesāsucipaggharaṇatāya, uppādajarābhaṅgehi uddhumātaparipakkapabhinnatāya ca gaṇḍato.
rows.english: as a boil because of being consequent upon impalement by suffering, because of oozing with the filth of defilements, and because of being swollen by arising, ripened by ageing, and burst by dissolution;

rows.pali: Pīḷājanakatāya, antotudanatāya, dunnīharaṇīyatāya ca sallato.
rows.english: as a dart because of producing oppression, because of penetrating inside, and because of being hard to extract;

rows.pali: Vigarahaṇīyatāya, avaḍḍhiāvahanatāya, aghavatthutāya ca aghato.
rows.english: as a calamity because of having to be condemned, because of bringing loss, and because of being the basis for calamity;

rows.pali: Aseribhāvajanakatāya, ābādhapadaṭṭhānatāya ca ābādhato.
rows.english: as an affliction because of restricting freedom, and because of being the foundation for affliction;

rows.pali: Avasatāya, avidheyyatāya ca parato.
rows.english: as alien because of inability to have mastery exercised over them, and because of intractability;

rows.pali: Byādhijarāmaraṇehi palujjanatāya palokato.
rows.english: as disintegrating because of crumbling through sickness, ageing and death;

rows.pali: Anekabyasanāvahanatāya ītito.
rows.english: as a plague because of bringing various kinds of ruin;

rows.pali: Aviditānaṃyeva vipulānaṃ anatthānaṃ āvahanato, sabbupaddavavatthutāya ca upaddavato.
rows.english: as a disaster because of bringing unforeseen and plentiful adversity,

rows.pali: Sabbabhayānaṃ ākaratāya, dukkhavūpasamasaṅkhātassa paramassāsassa paṭipakkhabhūtatāya ca bhayato.
rows.english: and because of being the basis for all kinds of terror, and because of being the opposite of the supreme comfort called the stilling of all suffering;

rows.pali: Anekehi anatthehi anubaddhatāya, dosūpasaṭṭhatāya, upasaggo viya anadhivāsanārahatāya ca upasaggato.
rows.english: as a menace because of being bound up with many kinds of adversity, because of being menaced5 by ills, and because of unfitness, as a menace, to be entertained;

rows.pali: Byādhijarāmaraṇehi ceva lābhālābhādīhi ca lokadhammehi pacalitatāya calato.
rows.english: as fickle because of fickle insecurity due to sickness, ageing and death, and to the worldly states of gain, etc.;6

rows.pali: Upakkamena ceva sarasena ca pabhaṅgupagamanasīlatāya pabhaṅguto.
rows.english: as perishable because of having the nature of perishing both by violence and naturally;

rows.pali: Sabbāvatthanipātitāya, thirabhāvassa ca abhāvatāya addhuvato.
rows.english: as unenduring because of collapsing on every occasion7 and because of lack of solidity;

rows.pali: Atāyanatāya ceva, alabbhaneyyakhematāya ca atāṇato.
rows.english: as no protection because of not protecting, and because of affording no safety;

rows.pali: Allīyituṃ anarahatāya, allīnānampi ca leṇakiccākāritāya aleṇato.
rows.english: as no shelter because of unfitness to give shelter,8 and because of not performing the function of a shelter for the unsheltered;9

rows.pali: Nissitānaṃ bhayasārakattābhāvena asaraṇato.
rows.english: as no refuge because of failure to disperse fear10 in those who depend on them;

rows.pali: Yathāparikappitehi dhuvasubhasukhattabhāvehi rittatāya rittato.
rows.english: as empty because of their emptiness of the lastingness, beauty, pleasure and self that are conceived about them;

rows.pali: Rittatāyeva tucchato appakattā vā, appakampi hi loke tucchanti vuccati.
rows.english: as vain because of their emptiness, or because of their triviality; for what is trivial is called “vain” in the world;

rows.pali: Sāmi-nivāsi-kāraka-vedakādhiṭṭhāyakavirahitatāya suññato.
rows.english: as void because devoid of the state of being an owner, abider, doer, experiencer, director;

rows.pali: Sayañca assāmikabhāvāditāya anattato.
rows.english: as not-self because of itself having no owner, etc.;

rows.pali: Pavattidukkhatāya, dukkhassa ca ādīnavatāya ādīnavato, atha vā ādīnaṃ vāti gacchati pavattatīti ādīnavo, kapaṇamanussassetaṃ adhivacanaṃ, khandhāpi ca kapaṇāyevāti ādīnavasadisatāya ādīnavato.
rows.english: as danger because of the suffering in the process of becoming, and because of the danger in suffering or, alternatively, as danger (ādīnava) because of resemblance to misery (ādīna)11 since “danger” (ādīnava) means that it is towards misery (ādīna) that it moves (vāti), goes, advances, this being a term for a wretched man, and the aggregates are wretched too;

rows.pali: Jarāya ceva maraṇena cāti dvedhā pariṇāmapakatitāya vipariṇāmadhammato.
rows.english: as subject to change because of having the nature of change in two ways, that is, through ageing and through death;

rows.pali: Dubbalatāya, pheggu viya sukhabhañjanīyatāya ca asārakato.
rows.english: as having no core because of feebleness, and because of decaying soon like sapwood;

rows.pali: Aghahetutāya aghamūlato.
rows.english: as the root of calamity because of being the cause of calamity;

rows.pali: Mittamukhasapatto viya vissāsaghātitāya vadhakato.
rows.english: as murderous because of breaking faith like an enemy posing as a friend;

rows.pali: Vigatabhavatāya, vibhavasambhūtatāya ca vibhavato.
rows.english: as due to be annihilated because their becoming disappears, and because their non-becoming comes about;

rows.pali: Āsavapadaṭṭhānatāya sāsavato.
rows.english: as subject to cankers because of being the proximate cause for cankers;

rows.pali: Hetupaccayehi abhisaṅkhatatāya saṅkhatato.
rows.english: as formed because of being formed by causes and conditions;

rows.pali: Maccumārakilesamārānaṃ āmisabhūtatāya mārāmisato.
rows.english: as Māra’s bait because of being the bait [laid] by the Māra of death and the Māra of defilement;

rows.pali: Jāti-jarā-byādhimaraṇapakatitāya jāti-jarā-byādhi-maraṇadhammato.
rows.english: as subject to birth, to ageing, to illness, and to death because of having birth, ageing, illness and death as their nature;

rows.pali: Soka-parideva-upāyāsahetutāya soka-paridevaupāyāsadhammato.
rows.english: as subject to sorrow, to lamentation and to despair because of being the cause of sorrow, lamentation and despair;

rows.pali: Taṇhādiṭṭhiduccaritasaṃkilesānaṃ visayadhammatāya saṃkilesikadhammatoti evaṃ pabhedato vuttassa aniccādisammasanassa vasena sammasati.
rows.english: as subject to defilement because of being the objective field of the defilements of craving, views and misconduct.

rows.pali: Ettha hi aniccato, palokato, calato, pabhaṅguto, addhuvato, vipariṇāmadhammato, asārakato, vibhavato, saṅkhatato, maraṇadhammatoti ekekasmiṃ khandhe dasa dasa katvā paññāsa aniccānupassanāni.
rows.english: 20.Now, there are fifty kinds of contemplation of impermanence here by taking the following ten in the case of each aggregate: as impermanent, as disintegrating, as fickle, as perishable, as unenduring, as subject to change, as having no core, as due to be annihilated, as formed, as subject to death.

rows.pali: Parato, rittato, tucchato, suññato, anattatoti ekekasmiṃ khandhe pañca pañca katvā pañcavīsati anattānupassanāni.
rows.english: There are twenty-five kinds of contemplation of not-self by taking the following five in the case of each aggregate: as alien, as empty, as vain, as void, as not-self.

rows.pali: Sesāni dukkhato, rogatotiādīni ekekasmiṃ khandhe pañcavīsati pañcavīsati katvā pañcavīsatisataṃ dukkhānupassanānīti.
rows.english: There are one hundred and twenty-five kinds of contemplation of pain by taking the rest beginning with “as painful, as a disease” in the case of each aggregate.

rows.pali: Iccassa iminā dvisatabhedena aniccādisammasanena pañcakkhandhe sammasato taṃ nayavipassanāsaṅkhātaṃ aniccadukkhānattasammasanaṃ thiraṃ hoti.
rows.english: So when a man comprehends the five aggregates by means of this comprehending as impermanent, etc., in its two hundred aspects, his comprehending as impermanent, painful and not-self, which is called “inductive insight,” is strengthened.

rows.pali: Idaṃ tāvettha pāḷinayānusārena sammasanārambhavidhānaṃ.
rows.english: These in the first place are the directions for undertaking comprehension here in accordance with the method given in the texts.

rows.pali: 699.Yassa pana evaṃ nayavipassanāya yogaṃ karotopi nayavipassanā na sampajjati, tena "navahākārehi indriyāni tikkhāni bhavanti – uppannuppannānaṃ saṅkhārānaṃ khayameva passati, tattha ca sakkaccakiriyāya sampādeti, sātaccakiriyāya sampādeti, sappāyakiriyāya sampādeti, samādhissa ca nimittaggāhena, bojjhaṅgānañca anupavattanatāya, kāye ca jīvite ca anapekkhataṃ upaṭṭhāpeti, tattha ca abhibhuyya nekkhammena, antarā ca abyosānenā"ti evaṃ vuttānaṃ navannaṃ ākārānaṃ vasena indriyāni tikkhāni katvā pathavīkasiṇaniddese vuttanayena satta asappāyāni vajjetvā satta sappāyāni sevamānena kālena rūpaṃ sammasitabbaṃ, kālena arūpaṃ.
rows.english: 21. While thus engaged in inductive insight, however, if it does not succeed, he should sharpen his faculties [of faith, etc.,] in the nine ways stated thus: “The faculties become sharp in nine ways: (1) he sees only the destruction of arisen formations; (2) and in that [occupation] he makes sure of working carefully, (3) he makes sure of working perseveringly, (4) he makes sure of working suitably, and (5) by apprehending the sign of concentration and (6) by balancing the enlightenment factors (7) he establishes disregard of body and life, (8) wherein he overcomes [pain] by renunciation and (9) by not stopping halfway.12 He should avoid the seven unsuitable things in the way stated in the Description of the Earth Kasiṇa (IV.34) and cultivate the seven suitable things, and he should comprehend the material at one time and the immaterial at another.

rows.pali: Rūpaṃ sammasantena rūpassa nibbatti passitabbā.
rows.english: 22. While comprehending materiality he should see how materiality is generated,13

rows.pali: 700.Seyyathidaṃ – idaṃ rūpaṃ nāma kammādivasena catūhi kāraṇehi nibbattati.
rows.english: that is to say, how this materiality is generated by the four causes beginning with kamma.

rows.pali: Tattha sabbesaṃ sattānaṃ rūpaṃ nibbattamānaṃ paṭhamaṃ kammato nibbattati.
rows.english: Herein, when materiality is being generated in any being, it is first generated from kamma.

rows.pali: Paṭisandhikkhaṇeyeva hi gabbhaseyyakānaṃ tāva tisantativasena vatthu-kāya-bhāvadasakasaṅkhātāni tiṃsa rūpāni nibbattanti, tāni ca kho paṭisandhicittassa uppādakkhaṇeyeva.
rows.english: For at the actual moment of rebirth- linking of a child in the womb, first thirty instances of materiality are generated in the triple continuity, in other words, the decads of physical [heart-]basis, body, and sex. And those are generated at the actual instant of the rebirth-linking consciousness’s arising.

rows.pali: Yathā ca uppādakkhaṇe, tathā ṭhitikkhaṇepi bhaṅgakkhaṇepi.
rows.english: And as at the instant of its arising, so too at the instant of its presence and at the instant of its dissolution.14

rows.pali: Tattha rūpaṃ dandhanirodhaṃ garuparivatti, cittaṃ khippanirodhaṃ lahuparivatti.
rows.english: 23. Herein, the cessation of materiality is slow and its transformation ponderous, while the cessation of consciousness is swift and its transformation quick (light);

rows.pali: Tenāha – "nāhaṃ, bhikkhave, aññaṃ ekadhammampi samanupassāmi, yaṃ evaṃ lahuparivattaṃ yathayidaṃ, bhikkhave, citta"nti (a. ni. 1.48).
rows.english: hence it is said, “Bhikkhus, I see no other one thing that is so quickly transformed as the mind” (A I 10).

rows.pali: Rūpe dharanteyeva hi soḷasavāre bhavaṅgacittaṃ uppajjitvā nirujjhati.
rows.english: 24.For the life-continuum consciousness arises and ceases sixteen times while one material instant endures.

rows.pali: Cittassa uppādakkhaṇopi ṭhitikkhaṇopi bhaṅgakkhaṇopi ekasadisā.
rows.english: With consciousness the instant of arising, instant of presence, and instant of dissolution are equal;

rows.pali: Rūpassa pana uppādabhaṅgakkhaṇāyeva lahukā, tehi sadisā.
rows.english: but with materiality only the instants of arising and dissolution are quick like those [of consciousness],

rows.pali: Ṭhitikkhaṇo pana mahā, yāva soḷasa cittāni uppajjitvā nirujjhanti, tāva vattati.
rows.english: while the instant of its presence is long and lasts while sixteen consciousnesses arise and cease.

rows.pali: Paṭisandhicittassa uppādakkhaṇe uppannaṃ ṭhānappattaṃ purejātaṃ vatthuṃ nissāya dutiyaṃ bhavaṅgaṃ uppajjati.
rows.english: 25. The second life-continuum arises with the prenascent physical [heart- ]basis as its support, which has already reached presence and arose at the rebirth-linking consciousness’s instant of arising.

rows.pali: Tena saddhiṃ uppannaṃ ṭhānappattaṃ purejātaṃ vatthuṃ nissāya tatiyaṃ bhavaṅgaṃ uppajjati.
rows.english: The third life-continuum arises with the prenascent physical basis as its support, which has already reached presence and arose together with that [second life-continuum consciousness].

rows.pali: Iminā nayena yāvatāyukaṃ cittappavatti veditabbā.
rows.english: The occurrence of consciousness can be understood to happen in this way throughout life.

rows.pali: Āsannamaraṇassa pana ekameva ṭhānappattaṃ purejātaṃ vatthuṃ nissāya soḷasa cittāni uppajjanti.
rows.english: But in one who is facing death sixteen consciousnesses arise with a single prenascent physical [heart-]basis as their support, which has already reached presence.

rows.pali: Paṭisandhicittassa uppādakkhaṇe uppannaṃ rūpaṃ paṭisandhicittato uddhaṃ soḷasamena cittena saddhiṃ nirujjhati.
rows.english: 26. The materiality that arose at the instant of arising of the rebirth-linking consciousness ceases along with the sixteenth consciousness after the rebirth- linking consciousness.

rows.pali: Ṭhānakkhaṇe uppannaṃ sattarasamassa uppādakkhaṇena saddhiṃ nirujjhati.
rows.english: That arisen at the instant of presence of the rebirth-linking consciousness ceases together with the instant of arising of the seventeenth.

rows.pali: Bhaṅgakkhaṇe uppannaṃ sattarasamassa ṭhānakkhaṇaṃ patvā nirujjhati.
rows.english: That arisen at the instant of its dissolution ceases on arriving at the instant of presence of the seventeenth.15

rows.pali: Yāva pavatti nāma atthi, evameva pavattati.
rows.english: It goes on occurring thus for as long as the recurrence [of births] continues.

rows.pali: Opapātikānampi sattasantativasena sattati rūpāni evameva pavattanti.
rows.english: Also seventy instances of materiality occur in the same way with the sevenfold continuity [beginning with the eye decad] of those apparitionally born.

rows.pali: 701.Tattha kammaṃ, kammasamuṭṭhānaṃ, kammapaccayaṃ, kammapaccayacittasamuṭṭhānaṃ, kammapaccayaāhārasamuṭṭhānaṃ, kammapaccayautusamuṭṭhānanti esa vibhāgo veditabbo.
rows.english: 27. Herein, [as regards kamma-born materiality] the analysis should be understood thus: (1) kamma, (2) what is originated by kamma, (3) what has kamma as its condition, (4) what is originated by consciousness that has kamma as its condition, (5) what is originated by nutriment that has kamma as its condition, (6) what is originated by temperature that has kamma as its condition (XI.111–14).

rows.pali: Tattha kammaṃ nāma kusalākusalacetanā.
rows.english: 28.Herein, (1) kamma is profitable and unprofitable volition.

rows.pali: Kammasamuṭṭhānaṃ nāma vipākakkhandhā ca, cakkhudasakādi samasattatirūpañca.
rows.english: (2) What is originated by kamma is the kamma-resultant aggregates and the seventy instances of materiality beginning with the eye decad.

rows.pali: Kammapaccayaṃ nāma tadeva, kammaṃ hi kammasamuṭṭhānassa upatthambhakapaccayopi hoti.
rows.english: (3) What has kamma as its condition is the same [as the last] since kamma is the condition that upholds what is originated by kamma.

rows.pali: Kammapaccayacittasamuṭṭhānaṃ nāma vipākacittasamuṭṭhānaṃ rūpaṃ.
rows.english: 29. (4) What is originated by consciousness that has kamma as its condition is materiality originated by kamma-resultant consciousness.

rows.pali: Kammapaccayaāhārasamuṭṭhānaṃ nāma kammasamuṭṭhānarūpesu ṭhānappattā ojā aññaṃ ojaṭṭhamakaṃ samuṭṭhāpeti, tatrāpi ojā ṭhānaṃ patvā aññanti evaṃ catasso vā pañca vā pavattiyo ghaṭeti.
rows.english: (5) What is originated by nutriment that has kamma as its condition is so called since the nutritive essence that has reached presence in the instances of materiality originated by kamma originates a further octad-with-nutritive-essence-as-eighth, and the nutritive essence there that has reached presence also originates a further one, and so it links up four or five occurrences of octads.

rows.pali: Kammapaccayautusamuṭṭhānaṃ nāma kammajatejodhātu ṭhānappattā utusamuṭṭhānaṃ ojaṭṭhamakaṃ samuṭṭhāpeti, tatrāpi utu aññaṃ ojaṭṭhamakanti evaṃ catasso vā pañca vā pavattiyo ghaṭeti.
rows.english: (6) What is originated by temperature that has kamma as its condition is so called since the kamma-born fire element that has reached presence originates an octad-with-nutritive-essence-as-eighth, which is temperature-originated, and the temperature in that originates a further octad-with- nutritive-essence-as eighth, and so it links up four or five occurrences of octads.

rows.pali: Evaṃ tāva kammajarūpassa nibbatti passitabbā.
rows.english: This is how the generation of kamma-born materiality in the first place should be seen.

rows.pali: 702.Cittajesupi cittaṃ, cittasamuṭṭhānaṃ, cittapaccayaṃ, cittapaccayaāhārasamuṭṭhānaṃ, cittapaccayautusamuṭṭhānanti esa vibhāgo veditabbo.
rows.english: 30. Also as regards the consciousness-born kinds, the analysis should be understood thus: (1) consciousness, (2) what is originated by consciousness, (3) what has consciousness as its condition, (4) what is originated by nutriment that has consciousness as its condition, (5) what is originated by temperature that has consciousness as its condition.

rows.pali: Tattha cittaṃ nāma ekūnanavuticittāni.
rows.english: 31.Herein, (1) consciousness is the eighty-nine kinds of consciousness.

rows.pali: Kāmāvacarato hi aṭṭha kusalāni, dvādasākusalāni, manodhātuvajjā dasa kiriyā, kusalakiriyato dve abhiññācittānīti dvattiṃsa cittāni rūpaṃ, iriyāpathaṃ, viññattiñca janenti.
rows.english: As regards the sense sphere, thirty-two consciousnesses, namely, the eight profitable consciousnesses ((1)–(8)), the twelve unprofitable ((22)–(33)), the ten functional excluding the mind element ((71)–(80)), and the two direct-knowledge consciousnesses as profitable and functional, give rise to materiality, to postures, and to intimation.

rows.pali: Vipākavajjāni sesadasarūpāvacarāni, aṭṭha arūpāvacarāni, aṭṭha lokuttaracittānīti chabbīsati cittāni rūpaṃ, iriyāpathañca janayanti, na viññattiṃ.
rows.english: The twenty-six consciousnesses, namely, the ten of the fine-material sphere ((9)–(13), (81)–(85)) and the eight of the immaterial sphere ((14)–(17), (86)– (89)) excluding the resultant [in both cases], and the eight supramundane ((18)– (21), (66)–(69)), give rise to materiality, to postures but not to intimation.

rows.pali: Kāmāvacare dasa bhavaṅgacittāni, rūpāvacare pañca, tisso manodhātuyo, ekā vipākāhetukamanoviññāṇadhātusomanassasahagatāti ekūnavīsati cittāni rūpameva janayanti, na iriyāpathaṃ, na viññattiṃ.
rows.english: The nineteen consciousnesses, namely, the ten life-continuum consciousnesses in the sense sphere ((41)–(49), (56)), the five in the fine-material sphere ((57)–(61)), the three mind elements ((39), (55), (70)), and the one resultant mind-consciousness element without root- cause and accompanied by joy (40), give rise to materiality only, not to postures or to intimation.

rows.pali: Dvepañcaviññāṇāni, sabbasattānaṃ paṭisandhicittaṃ, khīṇāsavānaṃ cuticittaṃ, cattāri āruppavipākānīti soḷasa cittāni neva rūpaṃ janayanti, na iriyāpathaṃ, na viññattiṃ.
rows.english: The sixteen consciousnesses, namely, the two sets of five consciousnesses ((34)–(38), (50)–(54)), the rebirth-linking consciousness of all beings, the death consciousness of those whose cankers are destroyed, and the four immaterial resultant consciousnesses ((62)–(65)) do not give rise to materiality or to postures or to intimation.

rows.pali: Yāni cettha rūpaṃ janenti, tāni na ṭhitikkhaṇe, bhaṅgakkhaṇe vā, tadā hi cittaṃ dubbalaṃ hoti.
rows.english: And those herein that do give rise to materiality do not do so at the instant of their presence or at the instant of their dissolution, for consciousness is weak then.

rows.pali: Uppādakkhaṇe pana balavaṃ, tasmā taṃ tadā purejātaṃ vatthuṃ nissāya rūpaṃ samuṭṭhāpeti.
rows.english: But it is strong at the instant of arising. Consequently it originates materiality then with the prenascent physical basis as its support.

rows.pali: Cittasamuṭṭhānaṃ nāma tayo arūpino khandhā, "saddanavakaṃ, kāyaviññatti, vacīviññatti, ākāsadhātu, lahutā, mudutā, kammaññatā, upacayo, santatī"ti sattarasavidhaṃ rūpañca.
rows.english: 32.(2) What is originated by consciousness is the three other immaterial aggregates and the seventeenfold materiality, namely, the sound ennead, bodily intimation, verbal intimation, the space element, lightness, malleability, wieldiness, growth, and continuity.

rows.pali: Cittapaccayaṃ nāma "pacchājātā cittacetasikā dhammā purejātassa imassa kāyassā"ti (paṭṭhā. 1.1.11) evaṃ vuttaṃ catusamuṭṭhānarūpaṃ.
rows.english: (3) What has consciousness as its condition is the materiality of fourfold origination stated thus: “Postnascent states of consciousness and consciousness- concomitants are a condition, as postnascence condition, for this prenascent body” (Paṭṭh I 5).

rows.pali: Cittapaccayaāhārasamuṭṭhānaṃ nāma cittasamuṭṭhānarūpesu ṭhānappattā ojā aññaṃ ojaṭṭhamakaṃ samuṭṭhāpeti, evaṃ dve tisso pavattiyo ghaṭeti.
rows.english: 33.(4) What is originated by nutriment that has consciousness as its condition: the nutritive essence that has reached presence in consciousness-originated material instances originates a further octad-with-nutritive-essence-as-eighth, and thus links up two or three occurrences of octads.

rows.pali: Cittapaccayautusamuṭṭhānaṃ nāma cittasamuṭṭhāno utu ṭhānappatto aññaṃ ojaṭṭhamakaṃ samuṭṭhāpeti, evaṃ dve tisso pavattiyo ghaṭeti.
rows.english: 34.(5) What is originated by temperature that has consciousness as its condition: the consciousness-originated temperature that has reached presence originates a further octad-with-nutritive-essence-as-eighth, and thus links up two or three occurrences.

rows.pali: Evaṃ cittajarūpassa nibbatti passitabbā.
rows.english: This is how the generation of consciousness-born materiality should be seen.

rows.pali: 703.Āhārajesupi āhāro, āhārasamuṭṭhānaṃ, āhārapaccayaṃ, āhārapaccayaāhārasamuṭṭhānaṃ, āhārapaccayautusamuṭṭhānanti esa vibhāgo veditabbo.
rows.english: 35. Also as regards the nutriment-born kinds, the analysis should be understood thus: (1) nutriment, (2) what is originated by nutriment, (3) what has nutriment as its condition, (4) what is originated by nutriment that has nutriment as its condition, (5) what is originated by temperature that has nutriment as its condition.

rows.pali: Tattha āhāro nāma kabaḷīkāro āhāro.
rows.english: 36.Herein, (1) nutriment is physical nutriment.

rows.pali: Āhārasamuṭṭhānaṃ nāma upādiṇṇaṃ kammajarūpaṃ paccayaṃ labhitvā tattha patiṭṭhāya ṭhānappattāya ojāya samuṭṭhāpitaṃ ojaṭṭhamakaṃ, ākāsadhātu, lahutā, mudutā, kammaññatā, upacayo, santatīti cuddasavidhaṃ rūpaṃ.
rows.english: (2) What is originated by nutriment is the fourteenfold materiality, namely, (i–viii) that of the octad-with-nutritive- essence-as-eighth originated by nutritive essence that has reached presence by obtaining as its condition kamma-born materiality that is clung to (kammically acquired) and basing itself on that,16 and (ix) space element, (x–xiv) lightness, malleability, wieldiness, growth, and continuity.

rows.pali: Āhārapaccayaṃ nāma "kabaḷīkāro āhāro imassa kāyassa āhārapaccayena paccayo"ti (paṭṭhā. 1.1.15) evaṃ vuttaṃ catusamuṭṭhānarūpaṃ.
rows.english: (3) What has nutriment as its condition is the materiality of fourfold origination stated thus: “Physical nutriment is a condition, as nutriment condition, for this body” (Paṭṭh I 5).

rows.pali: Āhārapaccayaāhārasamuṭṭhānaṃ nāma āhārasamuṭṭhānesu rūpesu ṭhānappattā ojā aññaṃ ojaṭṭhamakaṃ samuṭṭhāpeti, tatrāpi ojā aññanti evaṃ dasadvādasavāre pavattiṃ ghaṭeti.
rows.english: 37. (4) What is originated by nutriment that has nutriment as its condition: the nutritive essence that has reached presence in nutriment-originated material instances originates a further octad-with-nutritive-essence-as-eighth and the nutritive essence in that octad originates a further octad, and thus links up the occurrence of octads ten or twelve times.

rows.pali: Ekadivasaṃ paribhuttāhāro sattāhampi upatthambheti.
rows.english: Nutriment taken on one day sustains for as long as seven days;

rows.pali: Dibbā pana ojā ekamāsaṃ dvemāsampi upatthambheti.
rows.english: but divine nutritive essence sustains for as long as one or two months.

rows.pali: Mātarā paribhuttāhāropi dārakassa sarīraṃ pharitvā rūpaṃ samuṭṭhāpeti.
rows.english: The nutriment taken by a mother originates materiality by pervading the body of the child [in gestation].

rows.pali: Sarīre makkhitāhāropi rūpaṃ samuṭṭhāpeti.
rows.english: Also nutriment smeared on the body originates materiality.

rows.pali: Kammajāhāro upādiṇṇakāhāro nāma.
rows.english: Kamma-born nutriment is a name for nutriment that is clung to.

rows.pali: Sopi ṭhānappatto rūpaṃ samuṭṭhāpeti, tatrāpi ojā aññaṃ samuṭṭhāpetīti evaṃ catasso vā pañca vā pavattiyo ghaṭeti.
rows.english: That also originates materiality when it has reached presence. And the nutritive essence in it originates a further octad. Thus it links up four or five occurrences.

rows.pali: Āhārapaccayautusamuṭṭhānaṃ nāma āhārasamuṭṭhānā tejodhātu ṭhānappattā utusamuṭṭhānaṃ ojaṭṭhamakaṃ samuṭṭhāpeti.
rows.english: 38. (5) What is originated by temperature that has nutriment as its condition: nutriment-originated fire element that has reached presence originates an octad- with-nutritive-essence-as-eighth that is thus temperature-originated.

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ āhāro āhārasamuṭṭhānānaṃ janako hutvā paccayo hoti, sesānaṃ nissayāhāraatthiavigatavasenāti evaṃ āhārajarūpassa nibbatti passitabbā.
rows.english: Here this nutriment is a condition for nutriment-originated material instances as their progenitor. It is a condition for the rest as support, nutriment, presence, and non-disappearance. This is how the generation of nutriment-born materiality should be seen.

rows.pali: 704.Utujesupi utu, utusamuṭṭhānaṃ, utupaccayaṃ, utupaccayautusamuṭṭhānaṃ, utupaccayaāhārasamuṭṭhānanti esa vibhāgo veditabbo.
rows.english: 39. Also as regards the temperature-born kinds, the analysis should be understood thus: (1) temperature, (2) what is originated by temperature, (3) what has temperature as its condition, (4) what is originated by temperature that has temperature as its condition, (5) what is originated by nutriment that has temperature as its condition.

rows.pali: Tattha utu nāma catusamuṭṭhānā tejodhātu, uṇhautu sītautūti evaṃ panesa duvidho hoti.
rows.english: 40.Herein, (1) temperature is the fire element of fourfold origination; but it is twofold as hot temperature and cold temperature.

rows.pali: Utusamuṭṭhānaṃ nāma catusamuṭṭhāno utu upādiṇṇakaṃ paccayaṃ labhitvā ṭhānappatto sarīre rūpaṃ samuṭṭhāpeti.
rows.english: (2) What is originated by temperature: the temperature of fourfold origination that has reached presence by obtaining a clung-to condition originates materiality in the body.

rows.pali: Taṃ saddanavakaṃ, ākāsadhātu, lahutā, mudutā, kammaññatā, upacayo, santatīti pannarasavidhaṃ hoti.
rows.english: That materiality is fifteenfold, namely, sound ennead, space element, lightness, malleability, wieldiness, growth, continuity.

rows.pali: Utupaccayaṃ nāma utu catusamuṭṭhānikarūpānaṃ pavattiyā ca vināsassa ca paccayo hoti.
rows.english: (3) What has temperature as its condition is so called since temperature is a condition for the occurrence and for the destruction of materiality of fourfold origination.

rows.pali: Utupaccayautusamuṭṭhānaṃ nāma utusamuṭṭhānā tejodhātu ṭhānappattā aññaṃ ojaṭṭhamakaṃ samuṭṭhāpeti, tatrāpi utu aññanti evaṃ dīghampi addhānaṃ anupādiṇṇapakkhe ṭhatvāpi utusamuṭṭhānaṃ pavattatiyeva.
rows.english: 41.(4) What is originated by temperature that has temperature as its condition: the temperature-originated fire element that has reached presence originates a further octad-with-nutritive-essence-as-eighth, and the temperature in that octad originates a further octad. Thus temperature-originated materiality both goes on occurring for a long period and also maintains itself as well in what is not clung to.17

rows.pali: Utupaccayaāhārasamuṭṭhānaṃ nāma utusamuṭṭhānā ṭhānappattā ojā aññaṃ ojaṭṭhamakaṃ samuṭṭhāpeti, tatrāpi ojā aññanti evaṃ dasadvādasavāre pavattiṃ ghaṭeti.
rows.english: 42. (5) What is originated by nutriment that has temperature as its condition: the temperature-originated nutritive essence that has reached presence originates a further octad-with-nutritive-essence-as-eighth, and the nutritive essence in that originates a further one, thus it links up ten or twelve occurrences of octads.

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ utu utusamuṭṭhānānaṃ janako hutvā paccayo hoti, sesānaṃ nissayaatthiavigatavasenāti evaṃ utujarūpassa nibbatti passitabbā.
rows.english: Herein, this temperature is a condition for temperature-originated material instances as their progenitor. It is a condition for the rest as support, presence, and non-disappearance. This is how the generation of temperature-born materiality should be seen.

rows.pali: Evañhi rūpassa nibbattiṃ passanto kālena rūpaṃ sammasati nāma.
rows.english: One who sees the generation of materiality thus is said to “comprehend the material at one time” (§21).18

rows.pali: 705.Yathā ca rūpaṃ sammasantena rūpassa, evaṃ arūpaṃ sammasantenapi arūpassa nibbatti passitabbā.
rows.english: 43.And just as one who is comprehending the material should see the generation of the material, so too one who is comprehending the immaterial should see the generation of the immaterial.

rows.pali: Sā ca kho ekāsīti lokiyacittuppādavaseneva.
rows.english: And that is through the eighty-one mundane arisings of consciousness,

rows.pali: Seyyathidaṃ – idañhi arūpaṃ nāma purimabhave āyūhitakammavasena paṭisandhiyaṃ tāva ekūnavīsaticittuppādappabhedaṃ nibbattati.
rows.english: that is to say, it is by kamma accumulated in a previous becoming that this immaterial [mentality] is generated. And in the first place it is generated as [one of] the nineteen kinds of arisings of consciousness as rebirth-linking (XVII.130).

rows.pali: Nibbattanākāro panassa paṭiccasamuppādaniddese vuttanayeneva veditabbo.
rows.english: But the modes in which it is generated should be understood according to the method given in the Description of the Dependent Origination (XVII.134f.).

rows.pali: Tadeva paṭisandhicittassa anantaracittato paṭṭhāya bhavaṅgavasena, āyupariyosāne cutivasena.
rows.english: That same [nineteenfold arising of consciousness is generated] as life-continuum as well, starting from the consciousness next to rebirth-linking consciousness, and as death consciousness at the termination of the life span.

rows.pali: Yaṃ tattha kāmāvacaraṃ, taṃ chasu dvāresu balavārammaṇe tadārammaṇavasena.
rows.english: And when it is of the sense sphere, and the object in the six doors is a vivid one, it is also generated as registration.

rows.pali: Pavatte pana asambhinnattā cakkhussa āpāthagatattā rūpānaṃ ālokasannissitaṃ manasikārahetukaṃ cakkhuviññāṇaṃ nibbattati saddhiṃ sampayuttadhammehi.
rows.english: 44.In the course of an existence, eye-consciousness, together with its associated states, supported by light and caused by attention is generated because the eye is intact and because visible data have come into focus.

rows.pali: Cakkhupasādassa hi ṭhitikkhaṇe ṭhitippattameva rūpaṃ cakkhuṃ ghaṭṭeti.
rows.english: For it is actually when a visible datum has reached presence that it impinges on the eye at the instant of the eye- sensitivity’s presence.

rows.pali: Tasmiṃ ghaṭṭite dvikkhattuṃ bhavaṅgaṃ uppajjitvā nirujjhati.
rows.english: When it has done so, the life-continuum arises and ceases twice.

rows.pali: Tato tasmiṃyeva ārammaṇe kiriyamanodhātu āvajjanakiccaṃ sādhayamānā uppajjati.
rows.english: Next to arise is the functional mind element with that same object, accomplishing the function of adverting.

rows.pali: Tadanantaraṃ tadeva rūpaṃ passamānaṃ kusalavipākaṃ akusalavipākaṃ vā cakkhuviññāṇaṃ.
rows.english: Next to that, eye-consciousness, which is the result of profitable or of unprofitable [kamma] and sees that same visible datum.

rows.pali: Tato tadeva rūpaṃ sampaṭicchamānā vipākamanodhātu.
rows.english: Next, the resultant mind element, which receives that same visible datum.

rows.pali: Tato tadeva rūpaṃ santīrayamānā vipākāhetukamanoviññāṇadhātu.
rows.english: Next, the resultant root-causeless mind-consciousness element, which investigates that same visible datum.

rows.pali: Tato tadeva rūpaṃ vavatthāpayamānā kiriyāhetukamanoviññāṇadhātu upekkhāsahagatā.
rows.english: Next, the functional mind-consciousness element without root-cause and accompanied by equanimity, which determines that same visible datum.

rows.pali: Tato paraṃ kāmāvacarakusalākusalakiriyacittesu ekaṃ vā upekkhāsahagatāhetukaṃ cittaṃ pañca satta vā javanāni.
rows.english: Next, [it is generated either] as one from among the profitable ((l)–(8)), unprofitable ((22)–(33)), or functional ((71) and (73)–(80)) kinds of consciousness belonging to the sense sphere, either as consciousness accompanied by equanimity and without root-cause (71),19 or as five or seven impulsions.

rows.pali: Tato kāmāvacarasattānaṃ ekādasasu tadārammaṇacittesu javanānurūpaṃ yaṃkiñci tadārammaṇanti.
rows.english: Next, in the case of sense-sphere beings, [it is generated] as any of the eleven kinds of registration consciousness conforming [as to object] with the impulsions.

rows.pali: Esa nayo sesadvāresupi.
rows.english: The same applies to the remaining doors.

rows.pali: Manodvāre pana mahaggatacittānipi uppajjantīti.
rows.english: But in the case of the mind door-exalted consciousnesses also arise.

rows.pali: Evaṃ chasu dvāresu arūpassa nibbatti passitabbā.
rows.english: This is how the generation of the immaterial should be seen in the case of the six doors.

rows.pali: Evañhi arūpassa nibbattiṃ passanto kālena arūpaṃ sammasati nāma.
rows.english: One who sees the generation of the immaterial thus is said to “comprehend the immaterial at another time” (§21).

rows.pali: Evaṃ kālena rūpaṃ kālena arūpaṃ sammasitvāpi tilakkhaṇaṃ āropetvā anukkamena paṭipajjamāno eko paññābhāvanaṃ sampādeti.
rows.english: 45.This is how one [meditator] accomplishes the development of understand- ing, progressing gradually by comprehending at one time the material and at another time the immaterial, by attributing the three characteristics to them.

rows.pali: 706.Aparo rūpasattakaarūpasattakavasena tilakkhaṇaṃ āropetvā saṅkhāre sammasati.
rows.english: Another comprehends formations by attributing the three characteristics to them through the medium of the material septad and the immaterial septad.

rows.pali: Tattha ādānanikkhepanato, vayovuḍḍhatthaṅgamato, āhāramayato, utumayato, kammajato, cittasamuṭṭhānato, dhammatārūpatoti imehi ākārehi āropetvā sammasanto rūpasattakavasena āropetvā sammasati nāma.
rows.english: 46. Herein, one who comprehends [them] by attributing [the characteristics] in the following seven ways is said to comprehend by attributing through the medium of the material septad, that is to say, (1) as taking up and putting down, (2) as disappearance of what grows old in each stage, (3) as arising from nutriment, (4) as arising from temperature, (5) as kamma-born, (6) as consciousness-originated, and (7) as natural materiality.

rows.pali: Tenāhu porāṇā –
rows.english: Hence the Ancients said:

rows.pali: Tattha ādānanti paṭisandhi.
rows.english: 47. 1. Herein, taking up is rebirth-linking.

rows.pali: Nikkhepananti cuti.
rows.english: Putting down is death.

rows.pali: Iti yogāvacaro imehi ādānanikkhepehi ekaṃ vassasataṃ paricchinditvā saṅkhāresu tilakkhaṇaṃ āropeti.
rows.english: So the meditator allots one hundred years for this “taking up” and “putting down” and he attributes the three characteristics to formations.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: Etthantare sabbe saṅkhārā aniccā.
rows.english: All formations between these limits are impermanent.

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why?

rows.pali: Uppādavayavattito, vipariṇāmato, tāvakālikato, niccapaṭikkhepato ca.
rows.english: Because of the occurrence of rise and fall, because of change, because of temporariness, and because of preclusion of permanence.

rows.pali: Yasmā pana uppannā saṅkhārā ṭhitiṃ pāpuṇanti, ṭhitiyaṃ jarāya kilamanti, jaraṃ patvā avassaṃ bhijjanti, tasmā abhiṇhasampaṭipīḷanato, dukkhamato dukkhavatthuto, sukhapaṭikkhepato ca dukkhā.
rows.english: But since arisen formations have arrived at presence, and when present are afflicted by ageing, and on arriving at ageing are bound to dissolve, they are therefore painful because of continual oppression, because of being hard to bear, because of being the basis of suffering, and because of precluding pleasure.

rows.pali: Yasmā ca "uppannā saṅkhārā ṭhitiṃ mā pāpuṇantu, ṭhānappattā mā jīrantu, jarappattā mā bhijjantū"ti imesu tīsu ṭhānesu kassaci vasavattibhāvo natthi, suññā tena vasavattanākārena, tasmā suññato, assāmikato, avasavattito, attapaṭikkhepato ca anattāti.
rows.english: And since no one has any power over arisen formations in the three instances, “Let them not reach presence”, “Let those that have reached presence not age,” and “Let those that have reached ageing not dissolve,” and they are void of the possibility of any power being exercised over them, they are therefore not-self because void, because ownerless, because unsusceptible to the wielding of power, and because of precluding a self.20

rows.pali: 707.Evaṃ ādānanikkhepanavasena vassasataparicchinne rūpe tilakkhaṇaṃ āropetvā tato paraṃ vayovuḍḍhatthaṅgamato āropeti.
rows.english: 48.2. (a) Having attributed the three characteristics to materiality allotted one hundred years for the “taking up” and “putting down” thus, he next attributes them according to disappearance of what grows old in each stage.

rows.pali: Tattha vayovuḍḍhatthaṅgamo nāma vayavasena vuḍḍhassa vaḍḍhitassa rūpassa atthaṅgamo.
rows.english: Herein, “disappearance of what grows old in each stage” is a name for the disappearance of the materiality that has grown old during a stage [of life].

rows.pali: Tassa vasena tilakkhaṇaṃ āropetīti attho.
rows.english: The meaning is that he attributes the three characteristics by means of that.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: 49.How?

rows.pali: So tameva vassasataṃ paṭhamavayena majjhimavayena pacchimavayenāti tīhi vayehi paricchindati.
rows.english: He divides that same hundred years up into three stages, that is, the first stage, the middle stage, and the last stage.

rows.pali: Tattha ādito tettiṃsa vassāni paṭhamavayo nāma.
rows.english: Herein, the first thirty-three years are called the first stage,

rows.pali: Tato catuttiṃsa majjhimavayo nāma.
rows.english: the next thirty-four years are called the middle stage,

rows.pali: Tato tettiṃsa pacchimavayo nāmāti.
rows.english: and the next thirty-three years are called the last stage.

rows.pali: Iti imehi tīhi vayehi paricchinditvā, "paṭhamavaye pavattaṃ rūpaṃ majjhimavayaṃ appatvā tattheva nirujjhati, tasmā taṃ aniccaṃ.
rows.english: So after dividing it up into these three stages, [he attributes the three characteristics thus:] The materiality occurring in the first stage ceased there without reaching the middle stage: therefore it is impermanent;

rows.pali: Yadaniccaṃ, taṃ dukkhaṃ.
rows.english: what is impermanent is painful;

rows.pali: Yaṃ dukkhaṃ, tadanattā.
rows.english: what is painful is not-self.

rows.pali: Majjhimavaye pavattarūpampi pacchimavayaṃ appatvā tattheva nirujjhati, tasmā tampi aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā.
rows.english: Also the materiality occurring in the middle stage ceased there without reaching the last stage: therefore it is impermanent too and painful and not-self.

rows.pali: Pacchimavaye tettiṃsa vassāni pavattarūpampi maraṇato paraṃ gamanasamatthaṃ nāma natthi, tasmā tampi aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti tilakkhaṇaṃ āropeti.
rows.english: Also there is no materiality occurring in the thirty-three years of the last stage that is capable of out-lasting death: therefore that is impermanent too and painful and not-self. This is how he attributes the three characteristics.

rows.pali: 708.Evaṃ paṭhamavayādivasena vayovuḍḍhatthaṅgamato tilakkhaṇaṃ āropetvā puna "mandadasakaṃ, khiḍḍādasakaṃ, vaṇṇadasakaṃ, baladasakaṃ, paññādasakaṃ, hānidasakaṃ, pabbhāradasakaṃ, vaṅkadasakaṃ, momūhadasakaṃ, sayanadasaka"nti imesaṃ dasannaṃ dasakānaṃ vasena vayovuḍḍhatthaṅgamato tilakkhaṇaṃ āropeti.
rows.english: 50.2. (b) Having attributed the three characteristics according to “disappearance of what grows old in each stage” thus by means of the first stage, etc., he again attributes the three characteristics according to “disappearance of what grows old in each stage” by means of the following ten decades: the tender decade, the sport decade, the beauty decade, the strength decade, the understanding decade, the decline decade, the stooping decade, the bent decade, the dotage decade, and the prone decade.

rows.pali: Tattha dasakesu tāva vassasatajīvino puggalassa paṭhamāni dasa vassāni mandadasakaṃ nāma, tadā hi so mando hoti capalo kumārako.
rows.english: 51.Herein, as to these decades: in the first place, the first ten years of a person with a hundred years’ life are called the tender decade; for then he is a tender unsteady child.

rows.pali: Tato parāni dasa khiḍḍādasakaṃ nāma, tadā hi so khiḍḍāratibahulo hoti.
rows.english: The next ten years are called the sport decade; for he is very fond of sport then.

rows.pali: Tato parāni dasa vaṇṇadasakaṃ nāma, tadā hissa vaṇṇāyatanaṃ vepullaṃ pāpuṇāti.
rows.english: The next ten years are called the beauty decade; for his beauty reaches its full extent then.

rows.pali: Tato parāni dasa baladasakaṃ nāma, tadā hissa balañca thāmo ca vepullaṃ pāpuṇāti.
rows.english: The next ten years are called the strength decade; for his strength and power reach their full extent then.

rows.pali: Tato parāni dasa paññādasakaṃ nāma, tadā hissa paññā suppatiṭṭhitā hoti, pakatiyā kira dubbalapaññassāpi tasmiṃ kāle appamattakā paññā uppajjatiyeva.
rows.english: The next ten years are called the understanding decade; for his understanding is well established by then. Even in one naturally weak in understanding some understanding, it seems, arises at that time.

rows.pali: Tato parāni dasa hānidasakaṃ nāma, tadā hissa khiḍḍārativaṇṇabalapaññā parihāyanti.
rows.english: The next ten years are called the decline decade; for his fondness for sport and his beauty, strength, and understanding decline then.

rows.pali: Tato parāni dasa pabbhāradasakaṃ nāma, tadā hissa attabhāvo purato pabbhāro hoti.
rows.english: The next ten years are called the stooping decade; for his figure stoops forward then.

rows.pali: Tato parāni dasa vaṅkadasakaṃ nāma, tadā hissa attabhāvo naṅgalakoṭi viya vaṅko hoti.
rows.english: The next ten years are called the bent decade; for his figure becomes bent like the end of a plough then.

rows.pali: Tato parāni dasa momūhadasakaṃ nāma.
rows.english: The next ten years are called the dotage decade;

rows.pali: Tadā hi so momūho hoti, kataṃ kataṃ pamussati.
rows.english: for he is doting then and forgets what he does.

rows.pali: Tato parāni dasa sayanadasakaṃ nāma, vassasatiko hi sayanabahulova hoti.
rows.english: The next ten years are called the prone decade; for a centenarian mostly lies prone.

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ yogī etesaṃ dasakānaṃ vasena vayovuḍḍhatthaṅgamato tilakkhaṇaṃ āropetuṃ iti paṭisañcikkhati – "paṭhamadasake pavattarūpaṃ dutiyadasakaṃ appatvā tattheva nirujjhati, tasmā taṃ aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā.
rows.english: 52. Herein, in order to attribute the three characteristics according to “disappearance of what grows old in each stage” by means of these decades, the meditator considers thus: The materiality occurring in the first decade ceases there without reaching the second decade: therefore it is impermanent, painful, not-self.

rows.pali: Dutiyadasake - pe - navamadasake pavattarūpaṃ dasamadasakaṃ appatvā tattheva nirujjhati.
rows.english: The materiality occurring in the second decade … the materiality occurring in the ninth decade ceases there without reaching the tenth decade;

rows.pali: Dasamadasake pavattarūpaṃ punabbhavaṃ appatvā idheva nirujjhati, tasmā tampi aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti tilakkhaṇaṃ āropeti.
rows.english: the materiality occurring in the tenth decade ceases there without reaching the next becoming: therefore it is impermanent, painful, not-self. That is how he attributes the three characteristics.

rows.pali: 709.Evaṃ dasakavasena vayovuḍḍhatthaṅgamato tilakkhaṇaṃ āropetvā puna tadeva vassasataṃ pañcapañcavassavasena vīsatikoṭṭhāse katvā vayovuḍḍhatthaṅgamato tilakkhaṇaṃ āropeti.
rows.english: 53.2. (c) Having attributed the three characteristics according to “disappear- ance of what grows old in each stage” thus by means of the decades, he again attributes the three characteristics according to “disappearance of what grows old in each stage” by taking that same hundred years in twenty parts of five years each.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: 54. How?

rows.pali: So hi iti paṭisañcikkhati – "paṭhame vassapañcake pavattarūpaṃ dutiyaṃ vassapañcakaṃ appatvā tattheva nirujjhati, tasmā taṃ aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā.
rows.english: He considers thus: The materiality occurring in the first five years ceases there without reaching the second five years: therefore it is impermanent, painful, not-self.

rows.pali: Dutiye vassapañcake pavattarūpaṃ tatiyaṃ - pe - ekūnavīsatime vassapañcake pavattarūpaṃ vīsatimaṃ vassapañcakaṃ appatvā tattheva nirujjhati.
rows.english: The materiality occurring in the second five years … in the third … in the nineteenth five years ceases there without reaching the twentieth five years.

rows.pali: Vīsatime vassapañcake pavattarūpaṃ maraṇato paraṃ gamanasamatthaṃ nāma natthi, tasmā tampi aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti.
rows.english: There is no materiality occurring in the twentieth five years that is capable of outlasting death; therefore that is impermanent too, painful, not-self.

rows.pali: Evaṃ vīsatikoṭṭhāsavasena vayovuḍḍhatthaṅgamato tilakkhaṇaṃ āropetvā puna pañcavīsati koṭṭhāse katvā catunnaṃ catunnaṃ vassānaṃ vasena āropeti.
rows.english: 55.2. (d) Having attributed the three characteristics according to “disappear- ance of what grows old in each stage” thus by means of the twenty parts, he again attributes the three characteristics according to “disappearance of what grows old in each stage” by taking twenty-five parts of four years each.

rows.pali: Tato tettiṃsa koṭṭhāse katvā tiṇṇaṃ tiṇṇaṃ vassānaṃ vasena, paññāsa koṭṭhāse katvā dvinnaṃ dvinnaṃ vassānaṃ vasena, sataṃ koṭṭhāse katvā ekekavassavasena.
rows.english: (e) Next, by taking thirty-three parts of three years each, (f) by taking fifty parts of two years each, (g) by taking a hundred parts of one year each.

rows.pali: Tato ekaṃ vassaṃ tayo koṭṭhāse katvā vassānahemantagimhesu tīsu utūsu ekekautuvasena tasmiṃ vayovuḍḍhatthaṅgamarūpe tilakkhaṇaṃ āropeti.
rows.english: 2. (h) Next he attributes the three characteristics according to “disappearance of what grows old in each stage” by means of each of the three seasons, taking each year in three parts.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: 56.How?

rows.pali: "Vassāne catumāsaṃ pavattarūpaṃ hemantaṃ appatvā tattheva niruddhaṃ.
rows.english: The materiality occurring in the four months of the rains (vassāna) ceases there without reaching the winter (hemanta).

rows.pali: Hemante pavattarūpaṃ gimhaṃ appatvā tattheva niruddhaṃ.
rows.english: The materiality occurring in the winter ceases there without reaching the summer (gimha).

rows.pali: Gimhe pavattarūpaṃ puna vassānaṃ appatvā tattheva niruddhaṃ, tasmā taṃ aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti.
rows.english: The materiality occurring in the summer ceases there without reaching the rains again: therefore it is impermanent, painful, not-self.

rows.pali: Evaṃ āropetvā puna ekaṃ vassaṃ cha koṭṭhāse katvā – "vassāne dvemāsaṃ pavattarūpaṃ saradaṃ appatvā tattheva niruddhaṃ.
rows.english: 57.2. (i) Having attributed them thus, he again takes one year in six parts and attributes the three characteristics to this materiality according to “disappearance of what grows old in each stage” thus: The materiality occurring in the two months of the rains (vassāna) ceases there without reaching the autumn (sarada).

rows.pali: Sarade pavattarūpaṃ hemantaṃ.
rows.english: The materiality occurring in the autumn …

rows.pali: Hemante pavattarūpaṃ sisiraṃ.
rows.english: in the winter (hemanta) …

rows.pali: Sisire pavattarūpaṃ vasantaṃ.
rows.english: in the cool (sisira) …

rows.pali: Vasante pavattarūpaṃ gimhaṃ.
rows.english: in the spring (vasanta) …

rows.pali: Gimhe pavattarūpaṃ puna vassānaṃ appatvā tattheva niruddhaṃ, tasmā aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti evaṃ tasmiṃ vayovuḍḍhatthaṅgamarūpe tilakkhaṇaṃ āropeti.
rows.english: the materiality occurring in the summer (gimha) ceases there without reaching the rains again: therefore it is impermanent too, painful, not-self.

rows.pali: Evaṃ āropetvā tato kāḷajuṇhavasena – "kāḷe pavattarūpaṃ juṇhaṃ appatvā.
rows.english: 58.2. (j) Having attributed them thus, he next attributes the characteristics by means of the dark and bright halves of the moon thus: The materiality occurring in the dark half of the moon ceases there without reaching the bright half;

rows.pali: Juṇhe pavattarūpaṃ kāḷaṃ appatvā tattheva niruddhaṃ, tasmā aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti tilakkhaṇaṃ āropeti.
rows.english: the materiality occurring in the bright half ceases there without reaching the dark half: therefore it is impermanent, painful, not-self.

rows.pali: Tato rattindivavasena – "rattiṃ pavattarūpaṃ divasaṃ appatvā tattheva niruddhaṃ.
rows.english: 59.2. (k) Next he attributes the three characteristics by means of night and day thus: The materiality occurring in the night ceases there without reaching the day;

rows.pali: Divasaṃ pavattarūpampi rattiṃ appatvā tattheva niruddhaṃ, tasmā aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti tilakkhaṇaṃ āropeti.
rows.english: the materiality occurring in the day ceases there without reaching the night: therefore it is impermanent, painful, not-self.

rows.pali: Tato tadeva rattindivaṃ pubbaṇhādivasena cha koṭṭhāse katvā – "pubbaṇhe pavattarūpaṃ majjhanhaṃ appatvā.
rows.english: 60.2. (l) Next he attributes the three characteristics by taking that same day in six parts beginning with the morning thus: The materiality occurring in the morning ceased there without reaching the noon;

rows.pali: Majjhanhe pavattarūpaṃ sāyanhaṃ.
rows.english: the materiality occurring in the noon … without reaching the evening;

rows.pali: Sāyanhe pavattarūpaṃ paṭhamayāmaṃ.
rows.english: the materiality occurring in the evening … the first watch;

rows.pali: Paṭhamayāme pavattarūpaṃ majjhimayāmaṃ.
rows.english: the materiality occurring in the first watch … the middle watch;

rows.pali: Majjhimayāme pavattarūpaṃ pacchimayāmaṃ appatvā tattheva niruddhaṃ.
rows.english: the materiality occurring in the middle watch ceased there without reaching the last watch;

rows.pali: Pacchimayāme pavattarūpaṃ puna pubbaṇhaṃ appatvā tattheva niruddhaṃ, tasmā aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti tilakkhaṇaṃ āropeti.
rows.english: the materiality occurring in the last watch ceased there without reaching the morning again: therefore it is impermanent, painful, not- self.

rows.pali: 710.Evaṃ āropetvā puna tasmiṃyeva rūpe abhikkamapaṭikkamaālokanavilokanasamiñjanapasāraṇavasena – "abhikkame pavattarūpaṃ paṭikkamaṃ appatvā tattheva nirujjhati.
rows.english: 61. 2. (m) Having attributed them thus, he again attributes the three characteristics to that same materiality by means of moving forward and moving backward, looking toward and looking away, bending and stretching, thus: The materiality occurring in the moving forward ceases there without reaching the moving backward;

rows.pali: Paṭikkame pavattarūpaṃ ālokanaṃ.
rows.english: the materiality occurring in the moving backward … the looking toward;

rows.pali: Ālokane pavattarūpaṃ vilokanaṃ.
rows.english: the materiality occurring in the looking toward … the looking away;

rows.pali: Vilokane pavattarūpaṃ samiñjanaṃ.
rows.english: the materiality occurring in the looking away … the bending;

rows.pali: Samiñjane pavattarūpaṃ pasāraṇaṃ appatvā tattheva nirujjhati.
rows.english: the materiality occurring in the bending ceases there without reaching the stretching:

rows.pali: Tasmā aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti tilakkhaṇaṃ āropeti.
rows.english: therefore it is impermanent, painful, not-self (cf. M-a I 260).

rows.pali: Tato ekapadavāraṃ uddharaṇa atiharaṇavītiharaṇavossajjanasannikkhepanasannirumbhanavasena cha koṭṭhāse karoti.
rows.english: 62. 2. (n) Next he divides a single footstep into six parts as “lifting up,” “shifting forward,” “shifting sideways,” “lowering down,” “placing down,” and “fixing down21.”

rows.pali: Tattha uddharaṇaṃ nāma pādassa bhūmito ukkhipanaṃ.
rows.english: 63. Herein, lifting up is raising the foot from the ground.

rows.pali: Atiharaṇaṃ nāma purato haraṇaṃ.
rows.english: Shifting forward is shifting it to the front.

rows.pali: Vītiharaṇaṃ nāma khāṇukaṇṭakadīghajātiādīsu kiñcideva disvā ito cito ca pādasañcāraṇaṃ.
rows.english: Shifting sideways is moving the foot to one side or the other in seeing a thorn, stump, snake, and so on.

rows.pali: Vossajjanaṃ nāma pādassa heṭṭhā oropanaṃ.
rows.english: Lowering down is letting the foot down.

rows.pali: Sannikkhepanaṃ nāma pathavītale ṭhapanaṃ.
rows.english: Placing down is putting the foot on the ground.

rows.pali: Sannirumbhanaṃ nāma puna pāduddharaṇakāle pādassa pathaviyā saddhiṃ abhinippīḷanaṃ.
rows.english: Fixing down is pressing the foot on the ground while the other foot is being lifted up.

rows.pali: Tattha uddharaṇe pathavīdhātu āpodhātūti dve dhātuyo omattā honti mandā, itarā dve adhimattā honti balavatiyo.
rows.english: 64.Herein, in the lifting up two elements, the earth element and the water element, are subordinate22 and sluggish while the other two are predominant and strong.

rows.pali: Tathā atiharaṇavītiharaṇesu.
rows.english: Likewise in the shifting forward and shifting sideways.

rows.pali: Vossajjane tejodhātu vāyodhātūti dve dhātuyo omattā honti mandā, itarā dve adhimattā honti balavatiyo.
rows.english: In the lowering down two elements, the fire element and the air element, are subordinate and sluggish while the other two are predominant and strong.

rows.pali: Tathā sannikkhepanasannirumbhanesu.
rows.english: Likewise in the placing down and fixing down.

rows.pali: Evaṃ cha koṭṭhāse katvā tesaṃ vasena tasmiṃ vayovuḍḍhatthaṅgamarūpe tilakkhaṇaṃ āropeti.
rows.english: He attributes the three characteristics to materiality according to “disappearance of what grows old in each stage” by means of these six parts into which he has thus divided it.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: 65.How?

rows.pali: So iti paṭisañcikkhati – "yā uddharaṇe pavattā dhātuyo, yāni ca tadupādāyarūpāni, sabbe te dhammā atiharaṇaṃ appatvā ettheva nirujjhanti, tasmā aniccā dukkhā anattā.
rows.english: He considers thus: The elements and the kinds of derived materiality occurring in the lifting up all ceased there without reaching the shifting forward: therefore they are impermanent, painful, not-self.

rows.pali: Tathā atiharaṇe pavattā vītiharaṇaṃ.
rows.english: Likewise those occurring in the shifting forward … the shifting sideways;

rows.pali: Vītiharaṇe pavattā vossajjanaṃ.
rows.english: those occurring in the shifting sideways … the lowering down;

rows.pali: Vossajjane pavattā sannikkhepanaṃ.
rows.english: those occurring in the lowering down … the placing down;

rows.pali: Sannikkhepane pavattā sannirumbhanaṃ appatvā ettheva nirujjhanti.
rows.english: those occurring in the placing down cease there without reaching the fixing down;

rows.pali: Iti tattha tattha uppannā itaraṃ itaraṃ koṭṭhāsaṃ appatvā tattha tattheva pabbaṃ pabbaṃ sandhi sandhi odhi odhi hutvā tattakapāle pakkhittatilā viya taṭataṭāyantā saṅkhārā bhijjanti.
rows.english: thus formations keep breaking up, like crackling sesame seeds put into a hot pan; wherever they arise, there they cease stage by stage, section by section, term by term, each without reaching the next part:

rows.pali: Tasmā aniccā dukkhā anattā"ti.
rows.english: therefore they are impermanent, painful, not-self.

rows.pali: Tassevaṃ pabbapabbagate saṅkhāre vipassato rūpasammasanaṃ sukhumaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 66. When he sees formations stage by stage with insight thus, his comprehension of materiality has become subtle.

rows.pali: 711.Sukhumatte ca panassa idaṃ opammaṃ.
rows.english: Here is a simile for its subtlety.

rows.pali: Eko kira dārutiṇukkādīsu kataparicayo adiṭṭhapubbapadīpo paccantavāsiko nagaramāgamma antarāpaṇe jalamānaṃ padīpaṃ disvā ekaṃ purisaṃ pucchi ambho "kiṃ nāmetaṃ evaṃ manāpa"nti?
rows.english: A border dweller, it seems, who was familiar with torches of wood and grass, etc., but had never seen a lamp before, came to a city. Seeing a lamp burning in the market, he asked a man, “I say, what is that lovely thing called?”

rows.pali: Tamenaṃ so āha "kimettha manāpaṃ, padīpo nāmesa telakkhayena vaṭṭikkhayena ca gatamaggopissa na paññāyissatī"ti.
rows.english: —“What is lovely about that? It is called a lamp. Where it goes to when its oil and wick are used up no one knows.”

rows.pali: Tamañño evamāha "idaṃ oḷārikaṃ, imissā hi vaṭṭiyā anupubbena ḍayhamānāya tatiyabhāge tatiyabhāge jālā itarītaraṃ padesaṃ appatvāva nirujjhissatī"ti.
rows.english: Another told him, “That is crudely put; for the flame in each third portion of the wick as it gradually burns up ceases there without reaching the other parts.”

rows.pali: Tamañño evamāha "idampi oḷārikaṃ, imissā hi aṅgulaṅgulantare aḍḍhaṅgulaḍḍhaṅgulantare tantumhi tantumhi aṃsumhi aṃsumhi jālā itarītaraṃ aṃsuṃ appatvāva nirujjhissati.
rows.english: Other told him, “That is crudely put too; for the flame in each inch, in each half-inch, in each thread, in each strand, will cease without reaching the other strands;

rows.pali: Aṃsuṃ pana muñcitvā na sakkā jālaṃ paññāpetu"nti.
rows.english: but the flame cannot appear without a strand.”

rows.pali: Tattha "telakkhayena vaṭṭikkhayena ca padīpassa gatamaggopi na paññāyissatī"ti purisassa ñāṇaṃ viya yogino ādānanikkhepanato vassasatena paricchinnarūpe tilakkhaṇāropanaṃ.
rows.english: 67. Herein, the meditator’s attribution of the three characteristics to materiality delimited by the hundred years as “taking up” and “putting down” is like the man’s knowledge stated thus, “Where it goes when its oil and wick are used up no one knows.”

rows.pali: "Vaṭṭiyā tatiyabhāge tatiyabhāge jālā itarītaraṃ padesaṃ appatvāva nirujjhissatī"ti purisassa ñāṇaṃ viya yogino vassasatassa tatiyakoṭṭhāsaparicchinne vayovuḍḍhatthaṅgamarūpe tilakkhaṇāropanaṃ.
rows.english: The meditator’s attribution of the three characteristics according to “disappearance of what grows old in each stage” to the materiality delimited by the third part of the hundred years is like the man’s knowledge stated thus, “The flame in each third portion of the wick ceases without reaching the other parts.”

rows.pali: "Aṅgulaṅgulantare jālā itarītaraṃ appatvāva nirujjhissatī"ti purisassa ñāṇaṃ viya yogino dasavassa pañcavassa catuvassa tivassa dvivassa ekavassa paricchinne rūpe tilakkhaṇāropanaṃ.
rows.english: The meditator’s attribution of the three characteristics to materiality delimited by the periods of ten, five, four, three, two years, one year, is like the man’s knowledge stated thus, “The flame in each inch will cease without reaching the others.”

rows.pali: "Aḍḍhaṅgulaḍḍhaṅgulantare jālā itarītaraṃ appatvāva nirujjhissatī"ti purisassa ñāṇaṃ viya yogino ekekautuvasena ekaṃ vassaṃ tidhā, chadhā ca vibhajitvā catumāsa-dvimāsaparicchinne rūpe tilakkhaṇāropanaṃ.
rows.english: The meditator’s attribution of the three characteristics to materiality delimited by the four-month and two-month periods by classing the year as threefold and sixfold respectively according to the seasons is like the man’s knowledge stated thus, “The flame in each half-inch will cease without reaching the others.”

rows.pali: "Tantumhi tantumhi jālā itarītaraṃ appatvāva nirujjhissatī"ti purisassa ñāṇaṃ viya yogino kāḷajuṇhavasena, rattindivavasena, ekarattindivaṃ cha koṭṭhāse katvā pubbaṇhādivasena ca paricchinne rūpe tilakkhaṇāropanaṃ.
