rows.english: The meditator’s attribution of the three characteristics to materiality delimited by means of the dark and bright halves of the moon, by means of night and day, and by means of morning, etc., taking one night and day in six parts, is like the man’s knowledge stated thus, “The flame in each thread will cease without reaching the others.”

rows.pali: "Aṃsumhi aṃsumhi jālā itarītaraṃ appatvāva nirujjhissatī"ti purisassa ñāṇaṃ viya yogino abhikkamādivasena ceva uddharaṇādīsu ca ekekakoṭṭhāsavasena paricchinne rūpe tilakkhaṇāropananti.
rows.english: The meditator’s attribution of the three characteristics to materiality delimited by means of each part, namely, “moving forward,” etc., and “lifting up,” etc., is like the man’s knowledge stated thus, “The flame in each strand will cease without reaching the others.”

rows.pali: 712.So evaṃ nānākārehi vayovuḍḍhatthaṅgamarūpe tilakkhaṇaṃ āropetvā puna tadeva rūpaṃ visaṅkharitvā āhāramayādivasena cattāro koṭṭhāse katvā ekekakoṭṭhāse tilakkhaṇaṃ āropeti.
rows.english: 68. 3–6. Having in various ways thus attributed the three characteristics to materiality according to “disappearance of what grows old in each stage,” he analyzes that same materiality and divides it into four portions as “arising from nutriment,” etc., and he again attributes the three characteristics to each portion.

rows.pali: Tatrāssa āhāramayaṃ rūpaṃ chātasuhitavasena pākaṭaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 3. Herein, materiality arising from nutriment becomes evident to him through hunger and its satisfaction.

rows.pali: Chātakāle samuṭṭhitaṃ rūpaṃ hi jhattaṃ hoti kilantaṃ, jhāmakhāṇuko viya, aṅgārapacchiyaṃ nilīnakāko viya ca dubbaṇṇaṃ dussaṇṭhitaṃ.
rows.english: For materiality that is originated when one is hungry is parched and stale, and it is as ugly and disfigured as a parched stump, as a crow perching in a charcoal pit.

rows.pali: Suhitakāle samuṭṭhitaṃ dhātaṃ pīṇitaṃ mudu siniddhaṃ phassavantaṃ hoti.
rows.english: That originated when hunger is satisfied is plump, fresh, tender, smooth and soft to touch.

rows.pali: So taṃ pariggahetvā "chātakāle pavattarūpaṃ suhitakālaṃ appatvā ettheva nirujjhati.
rows.english: Discerning that:

rows.pali: Suhitakāle samuṭṭhitampi chātakālaṃ appatvā ettheva nirujjhati, tasmā taṃ aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti evaṃ tattha tilakkhaṇaṃ āropeti.
rows.english: The materiality occurring when hunger is satisfied ceases there without reaching the time when one is hungry; therefore it is impermanent, painful, not-self." he attributes the three characteristics to it thus (above).

rows.pali: 713.Utumayaṃ sītuṇhavasena pākaṭaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 69. 4. That arising from temperature becomes evident through cool and heat.

rows.pali: Uṇhakāle samuṭṭhitaṃ rūpaṃ hi jhattaṃ hoti kilantaṃ dubbaṇṇaṃ.
rows.english: For materiality that is originated when it is hot is parched, stale and ugly.

rows.pali: Sītautunā samuṭṭhitaṃ rūpaṃ dhātaṃ pīṇitaṃ siniddhaṃ hoti.
rows.english: Materiality originated by cool temperature is plump, fresh, tender, smooth, and soft to touch.

rows.pali: So taṃ pariggahetvā "uṇhakāle pavattarūpaṃ sītakālaṃ appatvā ettheva nirujjhati.
rows.english: Discerning that: "The materiality occurring when it is hot ceases there without reaching the time when it is cool.

rows.pali: Sītakāle pavattarūpaṃ uṇhakālaṃ appatvā ettheva nirujjhati, tasmā taṃ aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti evaṃ tattha tilakkhaṇaṃ āropeti.
rows.english: The materiality occurring when it is cool ceases there without reaching the time when it is hot: therefore it is impermanent, painful, not-self." he attributes the three characteristics to it thus (above).

rows.pali: 714.Kammajaṃ āyatanadvāravasena pākaṭaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 70.5. The kamma-born becomes evident through the sense doors, that is, the base [of consciousness].

rows.pali: Cakkhudvārasmiṃ hi cakkhukāyabhāvadasakavasena tiṃsa kammajarūpāni, upatthambhakāni pana tesaṃ utucittāhārasamuṭṭhānāni catuvīsatīti catupaṇṇāsa honti.
rows.english: For in the case of the eye door there are thirty material instances with decads of the eye, the body, and sex; but with the twenty-four instances originated by temperature, consciousness, and nutriment, [that is to say, three bare octads,] which are their support, there are fifty-four.

rows.pali: Tathā sotaghānajivhādvāresu.
rows.english: Likewise in the case of the doors of the ear, nose, and tongue.

rows.pali: Kāyadvāre kāyabhāvadasakavasena ceva utusamuṭṭhānādivasena ca catucattālīsa.
rows.english: In the case of the body door there are forty-four with the decads of body and sex and the instances originated by temperature, and so on.

rows.pali: Manodvāre hadayavatthukāyabhāvadasakavasena ceva utusamuṭṭhānādivasena ca catupaṇṇāsameva.
rows.english: In the case of the mind door there are fifty-four, too, with the decads of the heart-basis, the body, and sex, and those instances originated by the temperature, and so on.

rows.pali: So sabbampi taṃ rūpaṃ pariggahetvā "cakkhudvāre pavattarūpaṃ sotadvāraṃ appatvā ettheva nirujjhati.
rows.english: Discerning all that materiality, he attributes the three characteristics to it thus: The materiality occurring in the eye door ceases there without reaching the ear door;

rows.pali: Sotadvāre pavattarūpaṃ ghānadvāraṃ.
rows.english: the materiality occurring in the ear door … the nose door;

rows.pali: Ghānadvāre pavattarūpaṃ jivhādvāraṃ.
rows.english: the materiality occurring in the nose door … the tongue door;

rows.pali: Jivhādvāre pavattarūpaṃ kāyadvāraṃ.
rows.english: the materiality occurring in the tongue door … the body door;

rows.pali: Kāyadvāre pavattarūpaṃ manodvāraṃ appatvā ettheva nirujjhati, tasmā taṃ aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti evaṃ tattha tilakkhaṇaṃ āropeti.
rows.english: the materiality occurring in the body door ceases there without reaching the mind door: therefore it is impermanent, painful, not-self.

rows.pali: 715.Cittasamuṭṭhānaṃ somanassitadomanassitavasena pākaṭaṃ hoti, somanassitakāle uppannaṃ hi rūpaṃ siniddhaṃ mudu pīṇitaṃ phassavantaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 71.6. The consciousness-originated becomes evident through [the behaviour of] one who is joyful or grieved. For the materiality arisen at the time when he is joyful is smooth, tender, fresh and soft to touch.

rows.pali: Domanassitakāle uppannaṃ jhattaṃ kilantaṃ dubbaṇṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: That arisen at the time when he is grieved is parched, stale and ugly.

rows.pali: So taṃ pariggahetvā "somanassitakāle pavattarūpaṃ domanassitakālaṃ appatvā ettheva nirujjhati.
rows.english: Discerning that, he attributes the three characteristics to it thus: The materiality occurring at the time when one is joyful ceases there without reaching the time when one is grieved;

rows.pali: Domanassitakāle pavattarūpaṃ somanassitakālaṃ appatvā ettheva nirujjhati, tasmā taṃ aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti evaṃ tattha tilakkhaṇaṃ āropeti.
rows.english: the materiality occurring at the time when one is grieved ceases there without reaching the time when one is joyful: therefore it is impermanent, painful, not-self.

rows.pali: Tassevaṃ cittasamuṭṭhānarūpaṃ pariggahetvā tattha tilakkhaṇaṃ āropayato ayamattho pākaṭo hoti –
rows.english: 72. When he discerns consciousness-originated materiality and attributes the three characteristics to it in this way, this meaning becomes evident to him:

rows.pali: 716.Evaṃ āhāramayādīsu tilakkhaṇaṃ āropetvā puna dhammatārūpe tilakkhaṇaṃ āropeti.
rows.english: 73.7.Having attributed the three characteristics to that arising from nutriment, etc., he again attributes the three characteristics to natural materiality.

rows.pali: Dhammatārūpaṃ nāma bahiddhā anindriyabaddhaṃ ayalohatipusīsasuvaṇṇarajatamuttāmaṇiveḷuriyasaṅkhasilāpavāḷa-lohitaṅgamasāragallabhūmipāsāṇapabbatatiṇarukkhalatādibhedaṃ vivaṭṭakappato paṭṭhāya uppajjanakarūpaṃ.
rows.english: Natural materiality is a name for external materiality that is not bound up with faculties and arises along with the eon of world expansion, for example, iron, copper, tin, lead, gold, silver, pearl, gem, beryl, conch shell, marble, coral, ruby, opal, soil, stone, rock, grass, tree, creeper, and so on (see Vibh 83).

rows.pali: Tadassa asokaṅkurādivasena pākaṭaṃ hoti.
rows.english: That becomes evident to him by means of an asoka-tree shoot.

rows.pali: Asokaṅkuraṃ hi āditova tanurattaṃ hoti, tato dvīhatīhaccayena ghanarattaṃ, puna dvīhatīhaccayena mandarattaṃ, tato taruṇapallavavaṇṇaṃ, tato pariṇatapallavavaṇṇaṃ, tato haritapaṇṇavaṇṇaṃ.
rows.english: 74. For that to begin with is pale pink; then in two or three days it becomes dense red, again in two or three days it becomes dull red, next [brown,] the colour of a tender [mango] shoot; next, the colour of a growing shoot; next, the colour of pale leaves;

rows.pali: Tato nīlapaṇṇavaṇṇaṃ.
rows.english: next, the colour of dark green leaves.

rows.pali: Tato nīlapaṇṇavaṇṇakālato paṭṭhāya sabhāgarūpasantatimanuppabandhāpayamānaṃ saṃvaccharamattena paṇḍupalāsaṃ hutvā vaṇṭato chijjitvā patati.
rows.english: After it has become the colour of dark green leaves, as it follows out the successive stages of such material continuity, it eventually becomes withered foliage, and at the end of the year it breaks loose from its stem and falls off.

rows.pali: So taṃ pariggahetvā "tanurattakāle pavattarūpaṃ ghanarattakālaṃ appatvā nirujjhati.
rows.english: 75. Discerning that, he attributes the three characteristics to it thus: The materiality occurring when it is pale pink ceases there without reaching the time when it is dense red;

rows.pali: Ghanarattakāle pavattarūpaṃ mandarattakālaṃ.
rows.english: the materiality occurring when it is dense red … dull red;

rows.pali: Mandarattakāle pavattarūpaṃ taruṇapallavavaṇṇakālaṃ.
rows.english: the materiality occurring when it is dull red … the colour of a tender [mango] shoot;

rows.pali: Taruṇapallavavaṇṇakāle pavattaṃ pariṇatapallavavaṇṇakālaṃ.
rows.english: the materiality occurring when it is the colour of a tender [mango] shoot … the colour of a growing shoot;

rows.pali: Pariṇatapallavavaṇṇakāle pavattaṃ haritapaṇṇavaṇṇakālaṃ.
rows.english: the materiality occurring when it is the colour of a growing shoot … the colour of pale green leaves;

rows.pali: Haritapaṇṇakāle pavattaṃ nīlapaṇṇavaṇṇakālaṃ.
rows.english: the materiality occurring when it is the colour of pale green leaves … the colour of dark green leaves;

rows.pali: Nīlapaṇṇavaṇṇakāle pavattaṃ paṇḍupalāsakālaṃ.
rows.english: the materiality occurring when it is the colour of dark green leaves … the time when it is withered foliage;

rows.pali: Paṇḍupalāsakāle pavattaṃ vaṇṭato chijjitvā patanakālaṃ appatvāva nirujjhati, tasmā taṃ aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti tilakkhaṇaṃ āropeti, evaṃ tattha tilakkhaṇaṃ āropetvā iminā nayena sabbampi dhammatārūpaṃ sammasati.
rows.english: the materiality occurring when it is withered foliage ceases there without reaching the time when it breaks loose from its stem and falls off: therefore it is impermanent, painful, not-self. He comprehends all natural materiality in this way.

rows.pali: Evaṃ tāva rūpasattakavasena tilakkhaṇaṃ āropetvā saṅkhāre sammasati.
rows.english: This is how, firstly, he comprehends formations by attributing the three characteristics to them by means of the material septad.

rows.pali: 717.Yaṃ pana vuttaṃ "arūpasattakavasenā"ti, tattha ayaṃ mātikā – kalāpato, yamakato, khaṇikato, paṭipāṭito, diṭṭhiugghāṭanato, mānasamugghāṭanato, nikantipariyādānatoti.
rows.english: 76.The headings of what was called above “the immaterial septad” are these: (1) by groups, (2) by pairs, (3) by moments, (4) by series, (5) by removal of [false] view, (6) by abolition of conceit, (7) by ending of attachment.

rows.pali: Tattha kalāpatoti phassapañcamakā dhammā.
rows.english: 77.1.Herein, by groups means the states belonging to the contact pentad.24

rows.pali: Kathaṃ kalāpato sammasatīti?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: Idha bhikkhu iti paṭisañcikkhati – "ye ime 'kesā aniccā dukkhā anattā'ti sammasane uppannā phassapañcamakā dhammā, ye ca 'lomā - pe - matthaluṅgaṃ aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā'ti sammasane uppannā phassapañcamakā dhammā, sabbe te itarītaraṃ appatvā pabbaṃpabbaṃ odhiodhi hutvā tattakapāle pakkhittatilā viya taṭataṭāyantā vinaṭṭhā, tasmā aniccā dukkhā anattā"ti.
rows.english: Here, “he comprehends by groups” [means that] a bhikkhu considers thus: The states belonging to the contact pentad arising in the comprehending of head hairs as “impermanent, painful, not-self”; the states belonging to the contact pentad arising in the comprehending of body hairs as … in the contemplation of brain as “impermanent, painful, not-self”—all these states disintegrate section by section, term by term, like crackling sesame seeds put into a hot pan, each without reaching the next: therefore they are impermanent, painful, not-self.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ tāva visuddhikathāyaṃ nayo.
rows.english: This, firstly, is the method according to the Discourse on Purification.25

rows.pali: Ariyavaṃsakathāyaṃ pana "heṭṭhā rūpasattake sattasu ṭhānesu 'rūpaṃ aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā'ti pavattaṃ cittaṃ aparena cittena 'aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā'ti sammasanto 'kalāpato sammasatī'ti" vuttaṃ, taṃ yuttataraṃ.
rows.english: 78.According to the Discourse on the Noble Ones’ Heritages, however, he is said to “comprehend by groups” when by means of a subsequent consciousness he comprehends as “impermanent, painful, not-self” that consciousness which occurred [comprehending] materiality as “impermanent, painful, not-self” in the seven instances of the material septad given above.

rows.pali: Tasmā sesānipi teneva nayena vibhajissāma.
rows.english: As this method is more suitable we shall therefore confine ourselves to it in explaining the rest.

rows.pali: 718.Yamakatoti idha bhikkhu ādānanikkheparūpaṃ "aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti sammasitvā tampi cittaṃ aparena cittena "aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti sammasati.
rows.english: 79.2. By pairs: after the bhikkhu has comprehended as “impermanent, painful, not-self” the materiality of the “taking up and putting down” (§46f.), he comprehends that consciousness [with which he was comprehending the materiality] too as “impermanent, painful, not-self” by means of a subsequent consciousness.

rows.pali: Vayovuḍḍhatthaṅgamarūpaṃ, āhāramayaṃ, utumayaṃ, kammajaṃ, cittasamuṭṭhānaṃ, dhammatārūpaṃ "aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti sammasitvā tampi cittaṃ aparena cittena "aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti sammasati.
rows.english: After he has comprehended as “impermanent, painful, not-self” the materiality of the “disappearance of what grows old in each stage” and that “arising from nutriment,” “arising from temperature,” “kamma-born,” “consciousness-originated” and “natural,” he comprehends that consciousness too as “impermanent, painful, not-self” by means of a subsequent consciousness.

rows.pali: Evaṃ yamakato sammasati nāma.
rows.english: In this way he is said to comprehend by pairs.

rows.pali: 719.Khaṇikatoti idha bhikkhu ādānanikkheparūpaṃ "aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti sammasitvā taṃ paṭhamacittaṃ dutiyacittena, dutiyaṃ tatiyena, tatiyaṃ catutthena, catutthaṃ pañcamena "etampi aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti sammasati.
rows.english: 80.3. By moments: after the bhikkhu has comprehended as “impermanent, pain- ful, not-self” the materiality of the “taking up and putting down,” he comprehends that first consciousness [with which he was comprehending the materiality] as “impermanent, painful, not-self” by means of a second consciousness, and that second consciousness by means of a third, and the third by means of a fourth, and the fourth by means of a fifth, and that too he comprehends as “impermanent, painful, not-self.”

rows.pali: Vayovuḍḍhatthaṅgamarūpaṃ, āhāramayaṃ, utumayaṃ, kammajaṃ, cittasamuṭṭhānaṃ, dhammatārūpaṃ "aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti sammasitvā taṃ paṭhamacittaṃ dutiyacittena, dutiyaṃ tatiyena, tatiyaṃ catutthena, catutthaṃ pañcamena "etampi aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti sammasati.
rows.english: After he has comprehended as “impermanent, painful, not-self” the materiality of “disappearance of what grows old in each stage” and that “aris- ing from nutriment,” “arising from temperature,” “kamma-born,” “conscious- ness-originated” and “natural,” he comprehends that first consciousness as “im- permanent, painful, not-self” by means of a second consciousness, and that second consciousness by means of a third, and the third by means of a fourth, and the fourth by means of a fifth, and that too he comprehends as “impermanent, painful, not-self.”

rows.pali: Evaṃ rūpapariggāhakacittato paṭṭhāya cattāri cattāri cittāni sammasanto khaṇikato sammasati nāma.
rows.english: Comprehending thus four [consciousnesses] from each discerning of materiality he is said to comprehend by moments.

rows.pali: 720.Paṭipāṭitoti ādānanikkheparūpaṃ "aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti sammasitvā taṃ paṭhamacittaṃ dutiyacittena, dutiyaṃ tatiyena, tatiyaṃ catutthena - pe - dasamaṃ ekādasamena "etampi aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti sammasati.
rows.english: 81. 4. By series: after he has comprehended as “impermanent, painful, not- self” the materiality of the “taking up and putting down,” he comprehends that first consciousness as “impermanent, painful, not-self” by means of a second consciousness, and the second by means of a third, and the third by means of a fourth … and the tenth by means of an eleventh, and that too he comprehends as “impermanent, painful, not-self.”

rows.pali: Vayovuḍḍhatthaṅgamarūpaṃ, āhāramayaṃ, utumayaṃ, kammajaṃ, cittasamuṭṭhānaṃ, dhammatārūpaṃ "aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti sammasitvā taṃ paṭhamacittaṃ dutiyacittena, dutiyaṃ tatiyena, tatiyaṃ catutthena - pe - dasamaṃ ekādasamena "etampi aniccaṃ dukkhamanattā"ti evaṃ vipassanā paṭipāṭiyā sakalampi divasabhāgaṃ sammasituṃ vaṭṭeyya.
rows.english: After he has comprehended as “impermanent, painful, not-self” the materiality of the “disappearance of what grows old in each stage” and that “arising from nutriment,” “arising from temperature,” “kamma-born,” “consciousness-originated” and “natural,” he comprehends that consciousness as “impermanent, painful, not-self” by means of a second consciousness, and the second by means of a third, … and the tenth by means of an eleventh, and that too he comprehends as “impermanent, painful, not-self.” It would be possible to go on comprehending it in this way with serial insight even for a whole day.

rows.pali: Yāva dasamacittasammasanā pana rūpakammaṭṭhānampi arūpakammaṭṭhānampi paguṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: But both the material meditation subject and the immaterial meditation subject become familiar when the comprehending is taken as far as the tenth consciousness.

rows.pali: Tasmā dasameyeva ṭhapetabbanti vuttaṃ.
rows.english: That is why it is said26 that it can be stopped at the tenth.

rows.pali: Evaṃ sammasanto paṭipāṭito sammasati nāma.
rows.english: It is when he comprehends in this way that he is said to comprehend by series.

rows.pali: 721.Diṭṭhiugghāṭanato mānaugghāṭanato nikantipariyādānatoti imesu tīsu visuṃ sammasananayo nāma natthi.
rows.english: 82. 5. By removal of [false] view, 6. by abolition of conceit, 7. by ending of attachment: there is no individual method for any of these three.

rows.pali: Yaṃ panetaṃ heṭṭhā rūpaṃ, idha ca arūpaṃ pariggahitaṃ, taṃ passanto rūpārūpato uddhaṃ aññaṃ sattaṃ nāma na passati.
rows.english: But when he has discerned this materiality as described above and this immateriality as described here, then he sees that there is no living being over and above the material and the immaterial.

rows.pali: Sattassa adassanato paṭṭhāya sattasaññā ugghāṭitā hoti.
rows.english: As soon as he no longer sees a being, the perception of a being is removed.

rows.pali: Sattasaññaṃ ugghāṭitacittena saṅkhāre pariggaṇhato diṭṭhi nuppajjati.
rows.english: When he discerns formations with consciousness from which perception of a being has been removed, then [false] view does not arise in him.

rows.pali: Diṭṭhiyā anuppajjamānāya diṭṭhi ugghāṭitā nāma hoti.
rows.english: When [false] view does not arise in him, then [false] view is said to be removed.

rows.pali: Diṭṭhiugghāṭitacittena saṅkhāre pariggaṇhato māno nuppajjati.
rows.english: When he discerns formations with consciousness from which [false] view has been removed, then conceit does not arise in him.

rows.pali: Māne anuppajjante māno samugghāṭito nāma hoti.
rows.english: When conceit does not arise, conceit is said to be abolished.

rows.pali: Mānasamugghāṭitacittena saṅkhāre pariggaṇhato taṇhā nuppajjati.
rows.english: When he discerns formations with consciousness from which conceit has been abolished, then craving does not arise in him.

rows.pali: Taṇhāya anuppajjantiyā nikanti pariyādiṇṇā nāma hotīti idaṃ tāva visuddhikathāyaṃ vuttaṃ.
rows.english: When craving does not arise in him, attachment is said to be ended. This firstly is what is said in the Discourse on Purification.

rows.pali: Ariyavaṃsakathāyaṃpana "diṭṭhiugghāṭanato mānasamugghāṭanato nikantipariyādānato"ti mātikaṃ ṭhapetvā ayaṃ nayo dassito.
rows.english: 83.But in the Discourse on the Noble Ones’ Heritages, after setting forth the headings thus: “As removal of [false] view, as abolition of conceit, as ending of attachment,” the following method is set forth:

rows.pali: "Ahaṃ vipassāmi, mama vipassanā"ti gaṇhato hi diṭṭhisamugghāṭanaṃ nāma na hoti.
rows.english: “There is no removal of [false] view in one who takes it thus, ‘I see with insight, my insight’;

rows.pali: "Saṅkhārāva saṅkhāre vipassanti sammasanti vavatthapenti pariggaṇhanti paricchindantī"ti gaṇhato pana diṭṭhiugghāṭanaṃ nāma hoti.
rows.english: there is removal of [false] view in one who takes it thus, ‘Only formations see formations with insight, comprehend, define, discern, and delimit them.’

rows.pali: "Suṭṭhu vipassāmi, manāpaṃ vipassāmī"ti gaṇhato mānasamugghāṭo nāma na hoti.
rows.english: There is no abolition of conceit in one who takes it thus, ‘I see thoroughly with insight, I see well with insight’;

rows.pali: "Saṅkhārāva saṅkhāre vipassanti sammasanti vavatthapenti pariggaṇhanti paricchindantī"ti gaṇhato pana mānasamugghāṭo nāma hoti.
rows.english: there is abolition of conceit in one who takes it thus, ‘Only formations see formations with insight, comprehend, define, discern, and delimit them.’

rows.pali: "Vipassituṃ sakkomī"ti vipassanaṃ assādentassa nikantipariyādānaṃ nāma na hoti.
rows.english: There is no ending of attachment in one who is pleased with insight thus, ‘I am able to see with insight’;

rows.pali: "Saṅkhārāva saṅkhāre vipassanti sammasanti vavatthapenti pariggaṇhanti paricchindantī"ti gaṇhato pana nikantipariyādānaṃ nāma hoti.
rows.english: there is ending of attachment in one who takes it thus, ‘Only formations see formations with insight, comprehend, define, discern, and delimit them.’

rows.pali: Sace saṅkhārā attā bhaveyyuṃ, attāti gahetuṃ vaṭṭeyyuṃ, anattā ca pana attāti gahitā, tasmā te avasavattanaṭṭhena anattā, hutvā abhāvaṭṭhena aniccā, uppādavayapaṭipīḷanaṭṭhena dukkhāti passato diṭṭhiugghāṭanaṃ nāma hoti.
rows.english: 84.“There is removal of [false] view in one who sees thus: ‘If formations were self, it would be right to take them as self; but being not-self they are taken as self. Therefore they are not-self in the sense of no power being exercisable over them; they are impermanent in the sense of non-existence after having come to be; they are painful in the sense of oppression by rise and fall.’

rows.pali: Sace saṅkhārā niccā bhaveyyuṃ, niccāti gahetuṃ vaṭṭeyyuṃ, aniccā ca pana niccāti gahitā, tasmā te hutvā abhāvaṭṭhena aniccā, uppādavayapaṭipīḷanaṭṭhena dukkhā, avasavattanaṭṭhena anattāti passato mānasamugghāṭo nāma hoti.
rows.english: 85. “There is abolition of conceit in one who sees thus: ‘If formations were permanent, it would be right to take them as permanent; but being impermanent they are taken as permanent. Therefore they are impermanent in the sense of non-existence after having come to be; they are painful in the sense of oppression by rise and fall; they are not-self in the sense of no power being exercisable over them.’

rows.pali: Sace saṅkhārā sukhā bhaveyyuṃ, sukhāti gahetuṃ vaṭṭeyyuṃ, dukkhā ca pana sukhāti gahitā, tasmā te uppādavayapaṭipīḷanaṭṭhena dukkhā, hutvā abhāvaṭṭhena aniccā, avasavattanaṭṭhena anattāti passato nikantipariyādānaṃ nāma hoti.
rows.english: 86.“There is ending of attachment in one who sees thus: ‘If formations were pleasant, it would be right to take them as pleasant; but being painful they are taken as pleasant. Therefore they are painful in the sense of oppression by rise and fall; they are impermanent in the sense of non-existence after having come to be; they are not-self in the sense of no power being exercisable over them.’

rows.pali: Evaṃ saṅkhāre anattato passantassa diṭṭhisamugghāṭanaṃ nāma hoti.
rows.english: “Thus there comes to be the removal of [false] view in one who sees formations as not-self;

rows.pali: Aniccato passantassa mānasamugghāṭanaṃ nāma hoti.
rows.english: there comes to be the abolishing of conceit in one who sees them as impermanent;

rows.pali: Dukkhato passantassa nikantipariyādānaṃ nāma hoti.
rows.english: there comes to be the ending of attachment in one who sees them as painful.

rows.pali: Iti ayaṃ vipassanā attano attano ṭhāneyeva tiṭṭhatīti.
rows.english: So this insight is valid in each instance.”

rows.pali: Evaṃ arūpasattakavasenāpi tilakkhaṇaṃ āropetvā saṅkhāre sammasati.
rows.english: 88. This is how he comprehends formations by attributing the three characteristics to them by means of the immaterial septad.

rows.pali: Ettāvatā panassa rūpakammaṭṭhānampi arūpakammaṭṭhānampi paguṇaṃ hoti.
rows.english: At this stage both the material meditation subject and the immaterial meditation subject have become familiar to him.

rows.pali: 722.So evaṃ paguṇarūpārūpakammaṭṭhāno yā upari bhaṅgānupassanato paṭṭhāya pahānapariññāvasena sabbākārato pattabbā aṭṭhārasa mahāvipassanā, tāsaṃ idheva tāva ekadesaṃ paṭivijjhanto tappaṭipakkhe dhamme pajahati.
rows.english: 89.Having thus become familiar with the material and immaterial meditation subjects, and so having penetrated here already a part of those eighteen principal insights27 which are later on to be attained in all their aspects by means of full- understanding as abandoning starting with contemplation of dissolution, he consequently abandons things opposed [to what he has already penetrated].

rows.pali: Aṭṭhārasa mahāvipassanā nāma aniccānupassanādikā paññā.
rows.english: 90. Eighteen principal insights is a term for understanding that consists in the kinds of insight beginning with contemplation of impermanence.

rows.pali: Yāsu aniccānupassanaṃ bhāvento niccasaññaṃ pajahati, dukkhānupassanaṃ bhāvento sukhasaññaṃ pajahati, anattānupassanaṃ bhāvento attasaññaṃ pajahati, nibbidānupassanaṃ bhāvento nandiṃ pajahati, virāgānupassanaṃ bhāvento rāgaṃ pajahati, nirodhānupassanaṃ bhāvento samudayaṃ pajahati, paṭinissaggānupassanaṃ bhāvento ādānaṃ pajahati, khayānupassanaṃ bhāvento ghanasaññaṃ pajahati, vayānupassanaṃ bhāvento āyūhanaṃ pajahati, vipariṇāmānupassanaṃ bhāvento dhuvasaññaṃ pajahati, animittānupassanaṃ bhāvento nimittaṃ pajahati, appaṇihitānupassanaṃ bhāvento paṇidhiṃ pajahati, suññatānupassanaṃ bhāvento abhinivesaṃ pajahati, adhipaññādhammavipassanaṃ bhāvento sārādānābhinivesaṃ pajahati, yathābhūtañāṇadassanaṃ bhāvento sammohābhinivesaṃ pajahati, ādīnavānupassanaṃ bhāvento ālayābhinivesaṃ pajahati, paṭisaṅkhānupassanaṃ bhāvento appaṭisaṅkhaṃ pajahati, vivaṭṭānupassanaṃ bhāvento saṃyogābhinivesaṃ pajahati.
rows.english: Now, as regards these: (1) One who develops the contemplation of impermanence abandons the perception of permanence, (2) one who develops the contemplation of pain abandons the perception of pleasure, (3) one who develops the contemplation of not-self abandons the perception of self, (4) one who develops the contemplation of dispassion abandons delighting, (5) one who develops the contemplation of fading away abandons greed, (6) one who develops the contemplation of cessation abandons origination, (7) one who develops the contemplation of relinquishment abandons grasping, (8) one who develops the contemplation of destruction abandons the perception of compactness, (9) one who develops the contemplation of fall [of formations] abandons accumulation [of kamma], (10) one who develops the contemplation of change abandons the perception of lastingness, (11) one who develops the contemplation of the signless abandons the sign, (12) one who develops the contemplation of the desireless abandons desire, (13) one who develops the contemplation of voidness abandons misinterpreting (insistence), (14) one who develops the insight into states that is higher understanding abandons misinterpreting (insistence) due to grasping at a core, (15) one who develops correct knowledge and vision abandons misinterpreting (insistence) due to confusion, (16) one who develops the contemplation of danger abandons misinterpreting (insistence) due to reliance, (17) one who develops the contemplation of reflection abandons non-reflection, (18) one who develops the contemplation of turning away abandons misinterpreting (insistence) due to bondage (see Paṭis I 32f.).28

rows.pali: Tāsu yasmā iminā aniccādilakkhaṇattayavasena saṅkhārā diṭṭhā, tasmā anicca-dukkha-anattānupassanā paṭividdhā honti.
rows.english: 91.Now the meditator has seen formations by means of the three characteristics beginning with impermanence, and so he has therefore already penetrated among these eighteen insights the contemplations of impermanence, pain, and not-self.

rows.pali: Yasmā ca "yā ca aniccānupassanā yā ca animittānupassanā, ime dhammā ekatthā, byañjanameva nānaṃ".
rows.english: And then (1) the contemplation of impermanence and (11) the contemplation of the signless are one in meaning and different only in the letter,

rows.pali: Tathā "yā ca dukkhānupassanā yā ca appaṇihitānupassanā, ime dhammā ekatthā, byañjanameva nānaṃ".
rows.english: and so are (2) the contemplation of pain and (12) the contemplation of the desireless,

rows.pali: "Yā ca anattānupassanā yā ca suññatānupassanā, ime dhammā ekatthā, byañjanameva nāna"nti (paṭi. ma. 1.227) vuttaṃ.
rows.english: and so are (3) the contemplation of not-self and (13) the contemplation of voidness (see Paṭis II 63).

rows.pali: Tasmā tāpi paṭividdhā honti.
rows.english: Consequently these have been penetrated by him as well.

rows.pali: Adhipaññādhammavipassanā pana sabbāpi vipassanā.
rows.english: But (14) insight into states that is higher understanding is all kinds of insight,

rows.pali: Yathābhūtañāṇadassanaṃ kaṅkhāvitaraṇavisuddhiyā eva saṅgahitaṃ.
rows.english: and (15) correct knowledge and vision is included in purification by overcoming doubt (Ch. XIX).

rows.pali: Iti idampi dvayaṃ paṭividdhameva hoti.
rows.english: Consequently, these two have been penetrated by him as well.

rows.pali: Sesesu vipassanāñāṇesu kiñci paṭividdhaṃ, kiñci appaṭividdhaṃ, tesaṃ vibhāgaṃ parato āvikarissāma.
rows.english: As to the remaining kinds of insight, some have been penetrated and some not. We shall deal with them below.29

rows.pali: Yadeva hi paṭividdhaṃ, taṃ sandhāya idaṃ vuttaṃ "evaṃ paguṇarūpārūpakammaṭṭhāno yā upari bhaṅgānupassanato paṭṭhāya pahānapariññāvasena sabbākārato pattabbā aṭṭhārasa mahāvipassanā.
rows.english: 92. For it was with reference only to what has already been penetrated that it was said above: “having thus become familiar with the material and immaterial meditation subjects, and so having penetrated here already a part of those eighteen principal insights, which are later on to be attained in all their aspects by means of full understanding as abandoning starting with contemplation of dissolution,

rows.pali: Tāsaṃ idheva tāva ekadesaṃ paṭivijjhanto tappaṭipakkhe dhamme pajahatī"ti.
rows.english: he consequently abandons things opposed [to what he has already penetrated]” (§89).

rows.pali: 723.So evaṃ aniccānupassanādipaṭipakkhānaṃ niccasaññādīnaṃ pahānena visuddhañāṇo sammasanañāṇassa pāraṃ gantvā, yaṃ taṃ sammasanañāṇānantaraṃ "paccuppannānaṃ dhammānaṃ vipariṇāmānupassane paññā udayabbayānupassane ñāṇa"nti (paṭi. ma. mātikā 1.6) udayabbayānupassanaṃ vuttaṃ, tassa adhigamāya yogaṃ ārabhati.
rows.english: 93.Having purified his knowledge in this way by abandoning the perceptions of permanence, etc., which oppose the contemplations of impermanence, etc., he passes on from comprehension knowledge and begins the task of attaining that of contemplation of rise and fall, which is expressed thus: “Understanding [630] of contemplating present states’ change is knowledge of contemplation of rise and fall” (Paṭis I 1), and which comes next after comprehension knowledge.

rows.pali: Ārabhamāno ca saṅkhepato tāva ārabhati.
rows.english: 94.When he does so, he does it first in brief.

rows.pali: Tatrāyaṃ pāḷi –
rows.english: Here is the text:

rows.pali: So iminā pāḷinayena jātassa nāmarūpassa nibbattilakkhaṇaṃ jātiṃ uppādaṃ abhinavākāraṃ "udayo"ti, vipariṇāmalakkhaṇaṃ khayaṃ bhaṅgaṃ "vayo"ti samanupassati.
rows.english: 95. In accordance with the method of this text he sees the characteristic of generation, the birth, the arising, the aspect of renewal, of born materiality, as “rise,” and he sees its characteristic of change, its destruction, its dissolution, as “fall. ”

rows.pali: So evaṃ pajānāti "imassa nāmarūpassa uppattito pubbe anuppannassa rāsi vā nicayo vā natthi, uppajjamānassāpi rāsito vā nicayato vā āgamanaṃ nāma natthi, nirujjhamānassāpi disāvidisāgamanaṃ nāma natthi, niruddhassāpi ekasmiṃ ṭhāne rāsito nicayato nidhānato avaṭṭhānaṃ nāma natthi.
rows.english: 96. He understands thus: “There is no heap or store of unarisen mentality- materiality [existing] prior to its arising. When it arises, it does not come from any heap or store; and when it ceases, it does not go in any direction. There is nowhere any depository in the way of a heap or store or hoard of what has ceased.

rows.pali: Yathā pana vīṇāya vādiyamānāya uppannasaddassa neva uppattito pubbe sannicayo atthi, na uppajjamāno sannicayato āgato, na nirujjhamānassa disāvidisāgamanaṃ atthi, na niruddho katthaci sannicito tiṭṭhati, atha kho vīṇañca upavīṇañca purisassa ca tajjaṃ vāyāmaṃ paṭicca ahutvā sambhoti, hutvā paṭiveti.
rows.english: But just as there is no store, prior to its arising, of the sound that arises when a lute is played, nor does it come from any store when it arises, nor does it go in any direction when it ceases, nor does it persist as a store when it has ceased (cf. S IV 197), but on the contrary, not having been, it is brought into being owing to the lute, the lute’s neck, and the man’s appropriate effort, and having been, it vanishes—

rows.pali: Evaṃ sabbepi rūpārūpino dhammā ahutvā sambhonti, hutvā paṭiventī"ti.
rows.english: so too all material and immaterial states, not having been, are brought into being, and having been, they vanish.”

rows.pali: 724.Evaṃ saṅkhepato udayabbayamanasikāraṃ katvā puna yāni etasseva udayabbayañāṇassa vibhaṅge –
rows.english: 97. Having given attention to rise and fall in brief thus, he again [does so in detail according to condition and instant by seeing those characteristics] as given in the exposition of that same knowledge of rise and fall thus:

rows.pali: Vedanākkhandhassa viya ca saññāsaṅkhāraviññāṇakkhandhānaṃ.
rows.english: And as in the case of the feeling aggregate, [that is, substituting “contact” for the “nutriment” in the case of materiality,] so for the perception and formations aggregates.

rows.pali: Ayaṃ pana viseso, viññāṇakkhandhassa phassaṭṭhāne "nāmarūpasamudayā, nāmarūpanirodhā"ti –
rows.english: So also for the consciousness aggregate with this difference, that for the phrases containing “contact” there are substituted “with the arising of mentality-materiality” and “with the cessation of mentality-materiality. ”

rows.pali: Evaṃ ekekassa khandhassa udayabbayadassane dasa dasa katvā paññāsa lakkhaṇāni vuttāni.
rows.english: So there are fifty characteristics stated with the ten in the case of each aggregate by seeing rise and fall,

rows.pali: Tesaṃ vasena evampi rūpassa udayo evampi rūpassa vayo, evampi rūpaṃ udeti, evampi rūpaṃ vetīti paccayato ceva khaṇato ca vitthārena manasikāraṃ karoti.
rows.english: by means of which he gives attention in detail according to condition and according to instant (moment) in this way: “The rise of materiality is thus; its fall is thus; so it rises, so it falls.”

rows.pali: 725.Tassevaṃ manasikaroto "iti kirime dhammā ahutvā sambhonti, hutvā paṭiventī"ti ñāṇaṃ visadataraṃ hoti.
rows.english: 98. As he does so his knowledge becomes clearer thus: “So, it seems, these states, not having been, are brought into being; having been, they vanish. ”

rows.pali: Tassevaṃ paccayato ceva khaṇato ca dvedhā udayabbayaṃ passato saccapaṭiccasamuppādanayalakkhaṇabhedā pākaṭā honti.
rows.english: When he thus sees rise and fall in the two ways, according to condition and according to instant, the several truths, aspects of the dependent origination, methods, and characteristics become evident to him.

rows.pali: 726.Yañhi so avijjādisamudayā khandhānaṃ samudayaṃ, avijjādinirodhā ca khandhānaṃ nirodhaṃ passati, idamassa paccayato udayabbayadassanaṃ.
rows.english: 99.When he sees the arising of aggregates with the arising of ignorance and the cessation of aggregates with the cessation of ignorance, this is his seeing of rise and fall according to condition.

rows.pali: Yaṃ pana nibbattilakkhaṇavipariṇāmalakkhaṇāni passanto khandhānaṃ udayabbayaṃ passati, idamassa khaṇato udayabbayadassanaṃ, uppattikkhaṇeyeva hi nibbattilakkhaṇaṃ.
rows.english: When he sees the rise and fall of aggregates by seeing the characteristic of generation and the characteristic of change, this is his seeing of rise and fall according to instant. For it is only at the instant of arising that there is the characteristic of generation,

rows.pali: Bhaṅgakkhaṇe ca vipariṇāmalakkhaṇaṃ.
rows.english: and only at the instant of dissolution that there is the characteristic of change.

rows.pali: 727.Iccassevaṃ paccayato ceva khaṇato ca dvedhā udayabbayaṃ passato paccayato udayadassanena samudayasaccaṃ pākaṭaṃ hoti janakāvabodhato.
rows.english: 100.So when he sees rise and fall in the two ways, according to condition and according to instant thus, the truth of origination becomes evident to him through seeing rise according to condition owing to his discovery of the progenitor.

rows.pali: Khaṇato udayadassanena dukkhasaccaṃ pākaṭaṃ hoti jātidukkhāvabodhato.
rows.english: The truth of suffering becomes evident to him through seeing rise according to instant owing to his discovery of the suffering due to birth.

rows.pali: Paccayato vayadassanena nirodhasaccaṃ pākaṭaṃ hoti paccayānuppādena paccayavataṃ anuppādāvabodhato.
rows.english: The truth of cessation becomes evident to him through seeing fall according to condition owing to his discovery of the non-arising of things produced by conditions when their conditions do not arise.

rows.pali: Khaṇato vayadassanena dukkhasaccameva pākaṭaṃ hoti maraṇadukkhāvabodhato.
rows.english: The truth of suffering becomes evident to him too through seeing fall according to instant owing to his discovery of the suffering due to death.

rows.pali: Yañcassa udayabbayadassanaṃ, maggovāyaṃ lokikoti maggasaccaṃ pākaṭaṃ hoti tatra sammohavighātato.
rows.english: And his seeing of rise and fall becomes evident to him as the truth of the path thus: “This is the mundane path” owing to abolition of confusion about it.

rows.pali: 728.Paccayato cassa udayadassanena anulomo paṭiccasamuppādo pākaṭo hoti, "imasmiṃ sati idaṃ hotī"ti (ma. ni. 1.404; saṃ. ni. 2.21; udā. 1) avabodhato.
rows.english: 101.The dependent origination in forward order becomes evident to him through seeing rise according to condition owing to his discovery that “When this exists, that comes to be” (M I 262).

rows.pali: Paccayato vayadassanena paṭilomo paṭiccasamuppādo pākaṭo hoti, "imassa nirodhā idaṃ nirujjhatī"ti (ma. ni. 1.406; saṃ. ni. 2.21; udā. 2) avabodhato.
rows.english: The dependent origination in reverse order becomes evident to him through seeing fall according to condition owing to his discovery that “When this does not exist, that does not come to be” (M I 264).

rows.pali: Khaṇato pana udayabbayadassanena paṭiccasamuppannā dhammā pākaṭā honti saṅkhatalakkhaṇāvabodhato.
rows.english: Dependently- arisen states become evident to him through seeing rise and fall according to instant owing to his discovery of the characteristic of the formed;

rows.pali: Udayabbayavanto hi saṅkhatā, te ca paṭiccasamuppannāti.
rows.english: for the things possessed of rise and fall are formed and conditionally arisen.

rows.pali: 729.Paccayato cassa udayadassanena ekattanayo pākaṭo hoti hetuphalasambandhena santānassa anupacchedāvabodhato.
rows.english: 102.The method of identity becomes evident to him through seeing rise according to condition owing to his discovery of unbroken continuity in the connection of cause with fruit.

rows.pali: Atha suṭṭhutaraṃ ucchedadiṭṭhiṃ pajahati.
rows.english: Then he more thoroughly abandons the annihilation view.

rows.pali: Khaṇato udayadassanena nānattanayo pākaṭo hoti navanavānaṃ uppādāvabodhato.
rows.english: The method of diversity becomes evident to him through seeing rise according to instant owing to his discovery that each [state] is new [as it arises].

rows.pali: Atha suṭṭhutaraṃ sassatadiṭṭhiṃ pajahati.
rows.english: Then he more thoroughly abandons the eternity view.

rows.pali: Paccayato cassa udayabbayadassanena abyāpāranayo pākaṭo hoti dhammānaṃ avasavattibhāvāvabodhato.
rows.english: The method of uninterestedness becomes evident to him through seeing rise and fall according to condition owing to his discovery of the inability of states to have mastery exercised over them.

rows.pali: Atha suṭṭhutaraṃ attadiṭṭhiṃ pajahati.
rows.english: Then he more thoroughly abandons the self view.

rows.pali: Paccayato pana udayadassanena evaṃdhammatānayo pākaṭo hoti paccayānurūpena phalassa uppādāvabodhato.
rows.english: The method of ineluctable regularity becomes evident to him through seeing rise according to condition owing to his discovery of the arising of the fruit when the suitable conditions are there.

rows.pali: Atha suṭṭhutaraṃ akiriyadiṭṭhiṃ pajahati.
rows.english: Then he more thoroughly abandons the moral-inefficacy-of-action view.

rows.pali: 730.Paccayato cassa udayadassanena anattalakkhaṇaṃ pākaṭaṃ hoti dhammānaṃ nirīhakattapaccayapaṭibaddhavuttitāvabodhato.
rows.english: 103.The characteristic of not-self becomes evident to him through seeing rise according to condition owing to his discovery that states have no curiosity and that their existence depends upon conditions.

rows.pali: Khaṇato udayabbayadassanena aniccalakkhaṇaṃ pākaṭaṃ hoti hutvā abhāvāvabodhato, pubbantāparantavivekāvabodhato ca.
rows.english: The characteristic of impermanence becomes evident to him through seeing rise and fall according to instant owing to his discovery of non-existence after having been and owing to his discovery that they are secluded from past and future.

rows.pali: Dukkhalakkhaṇampi pākaṭaṃ hoti udayabbayehi paṭipīḷanāvabodhato.
rows.english: The characteristic of pain becomes evident to him [through that] too owing to his discovery of oppression by rise and fall.

rows.pali: Sabhāvalakkhaṇampi pākaṭaṃ hoti udayabbayaparicchinnāvabodhato.
rows.english: And the characteristic of individual essence becomes evident to him [through that] too owing to his discovery of delimitation [of states] by rise and fall.31

rows.pali: Sabhāvalakkhaṇe saṅkhatalakkhaṇassa tāvakālikattampi pākaṭaṃ hoti udayakkhaṇe vayassa, vayakkhaṇe ca udayassa abhāvāvabodhatoti.
rows.english: And in the characteristic of individual essence the temporariness of the characteristic of what is formed becomes evident to him [through that] too owing to his discovery of the non-existence of fall at the instant of rise and the non- existence of rise at the instant of fall. 32

rows.pali: 731.Tassevaṃ pākaṭībhūtasaccapaṭiccasamuppādanayalakkhaṇabhedassa "evaṃ kira nāmime dhammā anuppannapubbā uppajjanti, uppannā nirujjhantī"ti niccanavāva hutvā saṅkhārā upaṭṭhahanti.
rows.english: 104.When the several truths, aspects of the dependent origination, methods, and characteristics have become evident to him thus, then formations appear to him as perpetually renewed: “So these states, it seems, being previously unarisen, critic, and being arisen, they cease.”

rows.pali: Na kevalañca niccanavā, sūriyuggamane ussāvabindu viya udakabubbuḷo viya udake daṇḍarāji viya āragge sāsapo viya vijjuppādo viya ca parittaṭṭhāyino.
rows.english: And they are not only perpetually renewed, but they are also short-lived like dew-drops at sunrise (A IV 137), like a bubble on water (S III 14 I), like a line drawn on water (A IV 137), like a mustard seed on an awl’s point (Nidd I 42), like a lightning flash (Nidd I 43).

rows.pali: Māyāmarīcisupinantaalātacakkagandhabbanagarapheṇakadaliādayo viya assārā nissārāti cāpi upaṭṭhahanti.
rows.english: And they appear without core, like a conjuring trick (S III 141), like a mirage (Dhp 46), like a dream (Sn 807), like the circle of a whirling firebrand (source untraced), like a goblin city (source untraced), like froth (Dhp 46), like a plantain trunk (S III 142), and so on.

rows.pali: Ettāvatānena "vayadhammameva uppajjati, uppannañca vayaṃ upetī"ti iminā ākārena samapaññāsa lakkhaṇāni paṭivijjhitvā ṭhitaṃ udayabbayānupassanaṃ nāma taruṇavipassanāñāṇaṃ adhigataṃ hoti, yassādhigamā āraddhavipassakoti saṅkhaṃ gacchati.
rows.english: At this point he has attained tender insight-knowledge called contemplation of rise and fall, which has become established by penetrating the fifty characteristics in this manner: “Only what is subject to fall arises; and to be arisen necessitates fall. ” With the attainment of this he is known as a “beginner of insight. ”

rows.pali: 732.Athassa imāya taruṇavipassanāya āraddhavipassakassa dasa vipassanupakkilesā uppajjanti.
rows.english: 105. Now, when he is a beginner of insight with this tender insight, ten imperfections of insight arise in him.

rows.pali: Vipassanupakkilesā hi paṭivedhappattassa ariyasāvakassa ceva vippaṭipannakassa ca nikkhittakammaṭṭhānassa kusītapuggalassa nuppajjanti.
rows.english: For imperfections of insight do not arise either in a noble disciple who has reached penetration [of the truths] or in persons erring in virtue, neglectful of their meditation subject and idlers.

rows.pali: Sammāpaṭipannakassa pana yuttapayuttassa āraddhavipassakassa kulaputtassa uppajjantiyeva.
rows.english: They arise only in a clansman who keeps to the right course, devotes himself continuously [to his meditation subject] and is a beginner of insight.

rows.pali: Katame pana te dasa upakkilesāti?
rows.english: But what are these ten imperfections?

rows.pali: Obhāso, ñāṇaṃ, pīti, passaddhi, sukhaṃ, adhimokkho, paggaho, upaṭṭhānaṃ, upekkhā, nikantīti.
rows.english: They are: (1) illumination, (2) knowledge, (3) rapturous happiness, (4) tranquillity, (5) bliss (pleasure), (6) resolution, (7) exertion, (8) assurance, (9) equanimity, and (10) attachment.

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ –
rows.english: 106.For this is said:

rows.pali: "Kathaṃ dhammuddhaccaviggahitamānasaṃ hoti?
rows.english: “How does the mind come to be seized by agitation about higher states?

rows.pali: Aniccato manasikaroto obhāso uppajjati, 'obhāso dhammo'ti obhāsaṃ āvajjati, tato vikkhepo uddhaccaṃ.
rows.english: When a man is bringing [formations] to mind as impermanent, illumination arises in him. He adverts to the illumination thus, ‘Illumination is a [Noble One’s] state.’33 The distraction due to that is agitation.

rows.pali: Tena uddhaccena viggahitamānaso aniccato upaṭṭhānaṃ yathābhūtaṃ nappajānāti.
rows.english: When his mind is seized by that agitation, he does not understand correctly [their] appearance as impermanent,

rows.pali: Dukkhato… anattato upaṭṭhānaṃ yathābhūtaṃ nappajānāti".
rows.english: he does not understand correctly [their] appearance as painful, he does not understand correctly [their] appearance as not-self.

rows.pali: Tathā "aniccato manasikaroto ñāṇaṃ uppajjati - pe - pīti… passaddhi… sukhaṃ… adhimokkho… paggaho… upaṭṭhānaṃ… upekkhā… nikanti uppajjati, 'nikanti dhammo'ti nikantiṃ āvajjati, tato vikkhepo uddhaccaṃ.
rows.english: “Likewise, when he is bringing [formations] to mind as impermanent, knowledge arises in him … happiness … tranquillity … bliss … resolution … exertion … establishment … equanimity … attachment arises in him. He adverts to the attachment thus, ‘Attachment is a [Noble One’s] state.’ The distraction due to that is agitation.

rows.pali: Tena uddhaccena viggahitamānaso aniccato upaṭṭhānaṃ yathābhūtaṃ nappajānāti.
rows.english: When his mind is seized by that agitation, he does not correctly understand [their] appearance as impermanent,

rows.pali: Dukkhato… anattato upaṭṭhānaṃ yathābhūtaṃ nappajānātī"ti (paṭi. ma. 2.6).
rows.english: he does not correctly understand [their] appearance as painful, he does not correctly understand [their] appearance as not-self” (Paṭis II 100).

rows.pali: 733.Tattha obhāsoti vipassanobhāso.
rows.english: 107.1. Herein, illumination is illumination due to insight.34

rows.pali: Tasmiṃ uppanne yogāvacaro "na vata me ito pubbe evarūpo obhāso uppannapubbo, addhā maggappattosmi phalapattosmī"ti amaggameva "maggo"ti, aphalameva ca "phala"nti gaṇhāti.
rows.english: When it arises, the meditator thinks, “Such illumination never arose in me before. I have surely reached the path, reached fruition;” thus he takes what is not the path to be the path and what is not fruition to be fruition.

rows.pali: Tassa amaggaṃ "maggo"ti aphalaṃ "phala"nti gaṇhato vipassanāvīthi ukkantā nāma hoti.
rows.english: When he takes what is not the path to be the path and what is not fruition to be fruition, the course of his insight is interrupted.

rows.pali: So attano mūlakammaṭṭhānaṃ vissajjetvā obhāsameva assādento nisīdati.
rows.english: He drops his own basic meditation subject and sits just enjoying the illumination.

rows.pali: So kho panāyaṃ obhāso kassaci bhikkhuno pallaṅkaṭṭhānamattameva obhāsento uppajjati.
rows.english: 108.But this illumination arises in one bhikkhu illuminating only as much as the seat he is sitting on;

rows.pali: Kassaci antogabbhaṃ.
rows.english: in another, the interior of his room;

rows.pali: Kassaci bahigabbhampi.
rows.english: in another, the exterior of his room;

rows.pali: Kassaci sakalavihāraṃ, gāvutaṃ, aḍḍhayojanaṃ, yojanaṃ, dviyojanaṃ, tiyojanaṃ - pe - kassaci pathavītalato yāva akaniṭṭhabrahmalokā ekālokaṃ kurumāno.
rows.english: in another the whole monastery … a quarter league … a half league … a league … two leagues … three leagues; in another bhikkhu it arises making a single light from the earth’s surface up to the Brahmā-world.

rows.pali: Bhagavato pana dasasahassilokadhātuṃ obhāsento udapādi.
rows.english: But in the Blessed One it arose illuminating the ten-thousandfold world-element.

rows.pali: Evaṃ vemattatāya cassa idaṃ vatthu – cittalapabbate kira dvikuṭṭagehassa anto dve therā nisīdiṃsu.
rows.english: 109. This story illustrates how it varies. Two elders, it seems, were sitting inside a room with a double wall at Cittalapabbata.

rows.pali: Taṃdivasañca kāḷapakkhuposatho hoti, meghapaṭalacchannā disā, rattibhāge caturaṅgasamannāgataṃ tamaṃ pavattati.
rows.english: It was the Uposatha of the dark of the moon that day. All directions were covered by a blanket of cloud, and at night the four-factored gloom35 prevailed.

rows.pali: Atheko thero āha – "bhante, mayhaṃ idāni cetiyaṅgaṇamhi sīhāsane pañcavaṇṇāni kusumāni paññāyantī"ti.
rows.english: Then one elder said, “Venerable sir, the flowers of the five colours on the lion table on the shrine terrace are visible to me now.”

rows.pali: Taṃ itaro āha – "anacchariyaṃ, āvuso, kathesi, mayhaṃ panetarahi mahāsamuddamhi yojanaṭṭhāne macchakacchapā paññāyantī"ti.
rows.english: The other said, “What you say is nothing wonderful, friend. Actually the fishes and turtles in the ocean a league away are visible to me now.”

rows.pali: Ayaṃ pana vipassanupakkileso yebhuyyena samathavipassanālābhino uppajjati.
rows.english: 110.This imperfection of insight usually arises in one who has acquired serenity and insight.

rows.pali: So samāpattivikkhambhitānaṃ kilesānaṃ asamudācārato "arahā aha"nti cittaṃ uppādeti uccavālikavāsī mahānāgatthero viya haṃkanakavāsī mahādattatthero viya cittalapabbate niṅkapeṇṇakapadhānagharavāsī cūḷasumanatthero viya ca.
rows.english: Because the defilements suppressed by the attainments do not manifest themselves, he thinks, “I am an Arahant,” like the Elder Mahā-Nāga who lived at Uccavālika, like the Elder Mahā-Datta who lived at Haṅkana, like the Elder Cūḷa- Sumana who lived in the Nikapenna meditation house at Cittalapabbata.

rows.pali: Tatridaṃ ekavatthuparidīpanaṃ – talaṅgaravāsī dhammadinnatthero kira nāma eko pabhinnapaṭisambhido mahākhīṇāsavo mahato bhikkhusaṅghassa ovādadāyako ahosi.
rows.english: 111.Here is one story as an illustration. The Elder Dhammadinna, it seems, who lived at Talaṅgara—one of the great ones with cankers destroyed who possessed the categories of discrimination—was the instructor of a large community of bhikkhus.

rows.pali: So ekadivasaṃ attano divāṭṭhāne nisīditvā "kinnu kho amhākaṃ ācariyassa uccavālikavāsīmahānāgattherassa samaṇabhāvakiccaṃ matthakaṃ pattaṃ, no"ti āvajjanto puthujjanabhāvamevassa disvā "mayi agacchante puthujjanakālakiriyameva karissatī"ti ca ñatvā iddhiyā vehāsaṃ uppatitvā divāṭṭhāne nisinnassa therassa samīpe orohitvā vanditvā vattaṃ dassetvā ekamantaṃ nisīdi.
rows.english: One day, as he was sitting in his own daytime quarters, he wondered, “Has our teacher, the Elder Mahā-Nāga who lives at Uccavālika, brought his work of asceticism to its conclusion, or not? ” He saw that he was still an ordinary man, and he knew that if he did not go to him, he would die an ordinary man. He rose up into the air with supernormal power and alighted near the elder, who was sitting in his daytime quarters. He paid homage to him, doing his duty, and sat down at one side.

rows.pali: "Kiṃ, āvuso dhammadinna, akāle āgatosī"ti ca vutte "pañhaṃ, bhante, pucchituṃ āgatomhī"ti āha.
rows.english: To the question, “Why have you come unexpectedly, friend Dhammadinna?” he replied, “I have come to ask a question, venerable sir.”

rows.pali: Tato "pucchāvuso, jānamānā kathayissāmā"ti vutte pañhasahassaṃ pucchi.
rows.english: He was told, “Ask, friend. If we know, we shall say.” He asked a thousand questions.

rows.pali: Thero pucchitapucchitaṃ asajjamānova kathesi.
rows.english: 112.The elder replied without hesitation to each question.

rows.pali: Tato "atitikkhaṃ vo, bhante, ñāṇaṃ, kadā tumhehi ayaṃ dhammo adhigato"ti vutte "ito saṭṭhivassakāle, āvuso"ti āha.
rows.english: To the remark, “Your knowledge is very keen, venerable sir; when was this state attained by you?” he replied, “Sixty years ago, friend.”

rows.pali: Samādhimpi, bhante, vaḷañjethāti, na yidaṃ, āvuso, bhāriyanti.
rows.english: “Do you practice concentration, venerable sir?”— “That is not difficult, friend.”

rows.pali: Tena hi, bhante, ekaṃ hatthiṃ māpethāti.
rows.english: —“Then make an elephant, venerable sir.”

rows.pali: Thero sabbasetaṃ hatthiṃ māpesi.
rows.english: The elder made an elephant all white.

rows.pali: Idāni, bhante, yathā ayaṃ hatthī añcitakaṇṇo pasāritanaṅguṭṭho soṇḍaṃ mukhe pakkhipitvā bheravaṃ koñcanādaṃ karonto tumhākaṃ abhimukho āgacchati, tathā naṃ karothāti.
rows.english: “Now, venerable sir, make that elephant come straight at you with his ears outstretched, his tail extended, putting his trunk in his mouth and making a horrible trumpeting.” The elder did so.

rows.pali: Thero tathā katvā vegena āgacchato hatthissa bheravaṃ ākāraṃ disvā uṭṭhāya palāyituṃ āraddho.
rows.english: Seeing the frightful aspect of the rapidly approaching elephant, he sprang up and made to run away.

rows.pali: Tamenaṃ khīṇāsavatthero hatthaṃ pasāretvā cīvarakaṇṇe gahetvā "bhante, khīṇāsavassa sārajjaṃ nāma hotī"ti āha.
rows.english: Then the elder with cankers destroyed put out his hand, and catching him by the hem of his robe, he said, “Venerable sir, is there any timidity in one whose cankers are destroyed? ”

rows.pali: So tamhi kāle attano puthujjanabhāvaṃ ñatvā "avassayo me, āvuso, dhammadinna hohī"ti vatvā pādamūle ukkuṭikaṃ nisīdi.
rows.english: 113. Then he recognized that he was still an ordinary man. He knelt at Dhammadinna’s feet and said, “Help me, friend Dhammadinna.”

rows.pali: "Bhante, tumhākaṃ avassayo bhavissāmiccevāhaṃ āgato, mā cintayitthā"ti kammaṭṭhānaṃ kathesi.
rows.english: —“Venerable sir, I will help you; that is why I came. Do not worry.” Then he expounded a meditation subject to him.

rows.pali: Thero kammaṭṭhānaṃ gahetvā caṅkamaṃ āruyha tatiye padavāre aggaphalaṃ arahattaṃ pāpuṇi.
rows.english: The elder took the meditation subject and went up on to the walk, and with the third footstep he reached Arahantship.

rows.pali: Thero kira dosacarito ahosi.
rows.english: The elder was a bhikkhu of hating temperament, it seems.

rows.pali: Evarūpā bhikkhū obhāse kampanti.
rows.english: Such bhikkhus waver on account of illumination.

rows.pali: 734.Ñāṇanti vipassanāñāṇaṃ.
rows.english: 114.2. Knowledge is knowledge due to insight.

rows.pali: Tassa kira rūpārūpadhamme tulayantassa tīrentassa vissaṭṭhaindavajiramiva avihatavegaṃ tikhiṇaṃ sūraṃ ativisadaṃ ñāṇaṃ uppajjati.
rows.english: As he is estimating and judging material and immaterial states perhaps knowledge that is unerring, keen, incisive, and very sharp arises in him, like a lightning flash.

rows.pali: Pītīti vipassanāpīti.
rows.english: 115. 3. Rapturous happiness is happiness due to insight.

rows.pali: Tassa kira tasmiṃ samaye khuddakāpīti, khaṇikāpīti, okkantikāpīti, ubbegāpīti, pharaṇāpītīti ayaṃ pañcavidhā pīti sakalasarīraṃ pūrayamānā uppajjati.
rows.english: Perhaps at that time the five kinds of happiness, namely, minor happiness, momentary happiness, showering happiness, uplifting happiness, and pervading (rapturous) happiness arise in him filling his whole body.

rows.pali: Passaddhīti vipassanāpassaddhi.
rows.english: 116. 4. Tranquillity is tranquillity due to insight.

rows.pali: Tassa kira tasmiṃ samaye rattiṭṭhāne vā divāṭṭhāne vā nisinnassa kāyacittānaṃ neva daratho, na gāravaṃ, na kakkhaḷatā, na akammaññatā, na gelaññaṃ, na vaṅkatā hoti, atha kho panassa kāyacittāni passaddhāni lahūni mudūni kammaññāni suvisadāni ujukāniyeva honti.
rows.english: As he is sitting at that time in his night or day quarters perhaps there is no fatigue or heaviness or rigidity or unwieldiness or sickness or crookedness in his body and his mind, but rather his body and mind are tranquillized, light, malleable, wieldy, quite sharp, and straight.

rows.pali: So imehi passaddhādīhi anuggahitakāyacitto tasmiṃ samaye amānusiṃ nāma ratiṃ anubhavati.
rows.english: With his body and mind aided by this tranquillity, etc., he experiences at that time the superhuman delight

rows.pali: Yaṃ sandhāya vuttaṃ –
rows.english: with reference to which it is said:

rows.pali: Evamassa imaṃ amānusiṃ ratiṃ sādhayamānā lahutādisampayuttā passaddhi uppajjati.
rows.english: This is how tranquillity, associated with lightness, etc., arises in him, bringing about this superhuman delight.

rows.pali: Sukhanti vipassanāsukhaṃ.
rows.english: 117. 5. Bliss (pleasure) is bliss due to insight.

rows.pali: Tassa kira tasmiṃ samaye sakalasarīraṃ abhisandayamānaṃ atipaṇītaṃ sukhaṃ uppajjati.
rows.english: At that time perhaps there arises in him exceedingly refined bliss (pleasure) flooding his whole body.

rows.pali: Adhimokkhoti saddhā.
rows.english: 118. 6. Resolution is faith.

rows.pali: Vipassanāsampayuttāyeva hissa cittacetasikānaṃ atisayapasādabhūtā balavatī saddhā uppajjati.
rows.english: For strong faith arises in him in association with insight in the form of extreme confidence of consciousness and its concomitants.

rows.pali: Paggahoti vīriyaṃ.
rows.english: 119.7. Exertion is energy.

rows.pali: Vipassanāsampayuttameva hissa asithilaṃ anaccāraddhaṃ supaggahitaṃ vīriyaṃ uppajjati.
rows.english: For well-exerted energy, neither too lax nor too strained, arises in him in association with insight.

rows.pali: Upaṭṭhānanti sati.
rows.english: 120.8. Assurance (lit. establishment) is mindfulness.

rows.pali: Vipassanāsampayuttāyeva hissa supaṭṭhitā supatiṭṭhitā nikhātā acalā pabbatarājasadisā sati uppajjati.
rows.english: For well-established (well- assured), well-founded mindfulness, which is dug in and as immovable as the king of mountains, arises in him in association with insight.

rows.pali: So yaṃ yaṃ ṭhānaṃ āvajjati samannāharati manasikaroti paccavekkhati, taṃ taṃ ṭhānamassa okkhanditvā pakkhanditvā dibbacakkhuno paraloko viya satiyā upaṭṭhāti.
rows.english: Whatever subject he adverts to, consciously reacts to, gives attention to, reviews, appears to him (he is assured of) owing to mindfulness, which descends into it,36 enters into it, just as the other world does to one who has the divine eye.

rows.pali: Upekkhāti vipassanupekkhā ceva āvajjanupekkhā ca.
rows.english: 121.9. Equanimity is both equanimity about insight and equanimity in adverting.37

rows.pali: Tasmiṃ hissa samaye sabbasaṅkhāresu majjhattabhūtā vipassanupekkhāpi balavatī uppajjati.
rows.english: For equanimity about insight, which is neutrality about formations, arises strongly in him at that time.

rows.pali: Manodvāre āvajjanupekkhāpi.
rows.english: It is also equanimity in adverting in the mind door.

rows.pali: Sā hissa taṃ taṃ ṭhānaṃ āvajjantassa vissaṭṭhaindavajiramiva pattapuṭe pakkhitta tattanārāco viya ca sūrā tikhiṇā hutvā vahati.
rows.english: For whatever the subject he adverts to, his adverting works as incisively and sharply as a lightning flash, like a red-hot spear plunged into a basket of leaves.

rows.pali: Nikantīti vipassanānikanti.
rows.english: 122.10. Attachment is attachment due to insight.

rows.pali: Evaṃ obhāsādipaṭimaṇḍitāya hissa vipassanāya ālayaṃ kurumānā sukhumā santākārā nikanti uppajjati.
rows.english: For when his insight is adorned with illumination, etc., attachment arises in him, which is subtle and peaceful in aspect, and it relies on (clings to) that insight;

rows.pali: Yā nikanti kilesoti pariggahetumpi na sakkā hoti.
rows.english: and he is not able to discern that attachment as a defilement.

rows.pali: Yathā ca obhāse, evaṃ etesupi aññatarasmiṃ uppanne yogāvacaro "na vata me ito pubbe evarūpaṃ ñāṇaṃ uppannapubbaṃ, evarūpā pīti, passaddhi, sukhaṃ, adhimokkho, paggaho, upaṭṭhānaṃ, upekkhā, nikanti uppannapubbā, addhā maggappattosmi phalappattosmī"ti amaggameva "maggo"ti aphalameva ca "phala"nti gaṇhāti.
rows.english: 123.And as in the case of illumination, so too in the case of the other imperfections that may arise, the meditator thinks thus: “Such knowledge … such rapturous happiness … tranquillity … bliss … resolution … exertion … assurance … equanimity … attachment never arose in me before. I have surely reached the path, reached fruition.” Thus he takes what is not the path to be the path and what is not fruition to be fruition.

rows.pali: Tassa amaggaṃ "maggo"ti aphalaṃ "phala"nti gaṇhato vipassanāvīthi ukkantā nāma hoti.
rows.english: When he takes what is not the path to be the path and what is not fruition to be fruition, the course of his insight is interrupted.

rows.pali: So attano mūlakammaṭṭhānaṃ vissajjetvā nikantimeva assādento nisīdatīti.
rows.english: He drops his basic meditation subject and sits just enjoying the attachment.

rows.pali: 735.Ettha ca obhāsādayo upakkilesavatthutāya upakkilesāti vuttā, na akusalattā.
rows.english: 124. And here illumination, etc., are called imperfections because they are the basis for imperfection, not because they are [kammically] unprofitable.

rows.pali: Nikanti pana upakkileso ceva upakkilesavatthu ca.
rows.english: But attachment is both an imperfection and the basis for imperfection.

rows.pali: Vatthuvaseneva cete dasa.
rows.english: As basis only they amount to ten;

rows.pali: Gāhavasena pana samatiṃsa honti.
rows.english: but with the different ways of taking them they come to thirty.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
"Mama obhāso uppanno"ti gaṇhato hi diṭṭhigāho hoti, "manāpo vata obhāso uppanno"ti gaṇhato mānagāho, obhāsaṃ assādayato taṇhāgāho, iti obhāse diṭṭhimānataṇhāvasena tayo gāhā.
rows.english: When a man takes it thus, “illumination has arisen in me,” his way of taking is due to [false] view. When he takes it thus, “How agreeable this illumination that has arisen is,” his way of taking is due to pride (conceit). When he relishes the illumination, his way of taking is due to craving. So there are three ways of taking it in the case of illumination, that is to say, due to [false] view, to pride (conceit), and to craving.

rows.pali: Tathā sesesupīti evaṃ gāhavasena samatiṃsa upakkilesā honti.
rows.english: Likewise with the rest. So they come to thirty with the three ways of taking them.

rows.pali: Tesaṃ vasena akusalo abyatto yogāvacaro obhāsādīsu kampati vikkhipati.
rows.english: Owing to their influence an unskilful, unwary meditator wavers and gets distracted about illumination, etc.,

rows.pali: Obhāsādīsu ekekaṃ "etaṃ mama, esohamasmi, eso me attā"ti (ma. ni. 1.241) samanupassati.
rows.english: and he sees each one of them-illumination and the rest-as “This is mine, this is I, this is my self” (M I 135).

rows.pali: Tenāhu porāṇā –
rows.english: Hence the Ancients said:

rows.pali: 736.Kusalo pana paṇḍito byatto buddhisampanno yogāvacaro obhāsādīsu uppannesu "ayaṃ kho me obhāso uppanno, so kho panāyaṃ anicco saṅkhato paṭiccasamuppanno khayadhammo vayadhammo virāgadhammo nirodhadhammo"ti iti vā taṃ paññāya paricchindati upaparikkhati.
rows.english: 126. But when illumination, etc., arise, a skilful, wary meditator who is endowed with discretion either defines and examines it with understanding thus: “This illumination has arisen.38 But it is impermanent, formed, conditionally arisen, subject to destruction, subject to fall, subject to fading away, subject to cessation.”

rows.pali: Atha vā panassa evaṃ hoti, "sace obhāso attā bhaveyya, 'attā'ti gahetuṃ vaṭṭeyya.
rows.english: Or he thinks: “If illumination were self, it would be right to take it as self;

rows.pali: Anattā ca panāyaṃ 'attā'ti gahito.
rows.english: but being not-self, it is taken as self.

rows.pali: Tasmā so avasavattanaṭṭhena anattā, hutvā abhāvaṭṭhena anicco, uppādavayapaṭipīḷanaṭṭhena dukkho"ti sabbaṃ arūpasattake vuttanayena vitthāretabbaṃ.
rows.english: Therefore it is not-self in the sense of no power being exercisable over it; it is impermanent in the sense of non-existence after having come to be; it is painful in the sense of oppression by rise and fall,” all of which should be treated in detail according to the method given under the immaterial septad (§83).

rows.pali: Yathā ca obhāse, evaṃ sesesupi.
rows.english: And as in the case of illumination, so too with the rest.

rows.pali: So evaṃ upaparikkhitvā obhāsaṃ "netaṃ mama, nesohamasmi, na meso attā"ti (ma. ni. 1.241) samanupassati.
rows.english: 127.Having investigated it thus, he sees the illumination as “This is not mine, this is not I, this is not my self.”

rows.pali: Ñāṇaṃ - pe - nikantiṃ "netaṃ mama, nesohamasmi, na meso attā"ti (ma. ni. 1.241) samanupassati.
rows.english: He sees knowledge … (etc.) … attachment as “This is not mine, this is not I, this is not my self.”

rows.pali: Evaṃ samanupassanto obhāsādīsu na kampati na vedhati.
rows.english: Seeing thus, he does not waver or vacillate about the illumination, and so on.

rows.pali: Tenāhu porāṇā –
rows.english: Hence the Ancients said:

rows.pali: So evaṃ vikkhepaṃ agacchanto taṃ samatiṃsavidhaṃ upakkilesajaṭaṃ vijaṭetvā obhāsādayo dhammā na maggo.
rows.english: 128. So he unravels this thirtyfold skein of imperfections without falling a prey to wavering. “The states consisting in illumination, etc., are not the path;

rows.pali: Upakkilesavimuttaṃ pana vīthipaṭipannaṃ vipassanāñāṇaṃ maggoti maggañca amaggañca vavatthapeti.
rows.english: but it is insight knowledge that is free from imperfections and keeps to its course that is the path.” He defines what is the path and what is not the path thus (above).

rows.pali: Tassevaṃ "ayaṃ maggo, ayaṃ na maggo"ti maggañca amaggañca ñatvā ṭhitaṃ ñāṇaṃ maggāmaggañāṇadassanavisuddhīti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: 129. The knowledge that is established in him by his coming to know the path and the not-path thus, “This is the path, this is not the path,” should he understood as the purification by knowledge and vision of what is the path and what is not the path.

rows.pali: Ettāvatā ca pana tena tiṇṇaṃ saccānaṃ vavatthānaṃ kataṃ hoti.
rows.english: 130. So at this point the defining of three truths has been effected by him.

rows.pali: Kathaṃ?
rows.english: How?

rows.pali: Diṭṭhivisuddhiyaṃ tāva nāmarūpassa vavatthāpanena dukkhasaccassa vavatthānaṃ kataṃ.
rows.english: The defining of the truth of suffering has been effected with the defining of mentality-materiality in the purification of view.

rows.pali: Kaṅkhāvitaraṇavisuddhiyaṃ paccayapariggahaṇena samudayasaccassa vavatthānaṃ.
rows.english: The defining of the truth of origination has been effected with the discerning of conditions in the purification by overcoming doubt.

rows.pali: Imissaṃ maggāmaggañāṇadassanavisuddhiyaṃ sammāmaggassa avadhāraṇena maggasaccassa vavatthānaṃ katanti, evaṃ lokiyeneva tāva ñāṇena tiṇṇaṃ saccānaṃ vavatthānaṃ kataṃ hoti.
rows.english: The defining of the truth of the path has been effected with the emphasizing of the right path in this purification by knowledge and vision of what is the path and what is not the path. So the defining of three truths has been effected firstly by means of mundane knowledge only.

rows.pali: 692.Ayaṃ maggo, ayaṃ na maggoti evaṃ maggañca amaggañca ñatvā ṭhitaṃ ñāṇaṃ pana maggāmaggañāṇadassanavisuddhi nāma.
rows.english: 1.The knowledge established by getting to know the path and the not- path thus, “This is the path, this is not the path,” is called “purification by knowledge and vision of what is the path and what is not the path.”

rows.pali: Taṃ sampādetukāmena kalāpasammasanasaṅkhātāya nayavipassanāya tāva yogo karaṇīyo.
rows.english: 2.One who desires to accomplish this should first of all apply himself to the inductive insight called “comprehension by groups.1”

rows.pali: Kasmā?
rows.english: Why?

rows.pali: Āraddhavipassakassa obhāsādisambhave maggāmaggañāṇasambhavato.
rows.english: Because knowledge of what is the path and what is not the path appears in connection with the appearance of illumination, etc. (XX.105f.) in one who has begun insight.

rows.pali: Āraddhavipassakassa hi obhāsādīsu sambhūtesu maggāmaggañāṇaṃ hoti, vipassanāya ca kalāpasammasanaṃ ādi.
rows.english: For it is after illumination, etc., have appeared in one who has already begun insight that there comes to be knowledge of what is the path and what is not the path. And comprehension by groups is the beginning of insight.

rows.pali: Tasmā etaṃ kaṅkhāvitaraṇānantaraṃ uddiṭṭhaṃ.
rows.english: That is why it is set forth next to the overcoming of doubt.

rows.pali: Apica yasmā tīraṇapariññāya vattamānāya maggāmaggañāṇaṃ uppajjati, tīraṇapariññā ca ñātapariññānantarā, tasmāpi taṃ maggāmaggañāṇadassanavisuddhiṃ sampādetukāmena kalāpasammasane tāva yogo kātabbo.
rows.english: Besides, knowledge of what is the path and what is not the path arises when “full-understanding as investigation” is occurring, and full-understanding as investigation comes next to full- understanding as the known (see XIX.21). So this is also a reason why one who desires to accomplish this purification by knowledge and vision of what is the path and what is not the path should first of all apply himself to comprehension by groups.

rows.pali: 693.Tatrāyaṃ vinicchayo – tisso hi lokiyapariññā ñātapariññā tīraṇapariññā pahānapariññā ca.
rows.english: 3.Here is the exposition: there are three kinds of mundane full-understanding, that is, full-understanding as the known, full-understanding as investigation, and full-understanding as abandoning,

rows.pali: Yā sandhāya vuttaṃ "abhiññāpaññā ñātaṭṭhe ñāṇaṃ.
rows.english: with reference to which it was said: “Understanding that is direct-knowledge is knowledge in the sense of being known.

rows.pali: Pariññāpaññā tīraṇaṭṭhe ñāṇaṃ.
rows.english: Understanding that is full-understanding is knowledge in the sense of investigating.

rows.pali: Pahānapaññā pariccāgaṭṭhe ñāṇa"nti (paṭi. ma. 1.75).
rows.english: Understanding that is abandoning is knowledge in the sense of giving up” (Paṭis I 87).

rows.pali: Tattha "ruppanalakkhaṇaṃ rūpaṃ, vedayitalakkhaṇā vedanā"ti evaṃ tesaṃ tesaṃ dhammānaṃ paccattalakkhaṇasallakkhaṇavasena pavattā paññā ñātapariññā nāma.
rows.english: Herein, the understanding that occurs by observing the specific characteristics of such and such states thus, “Materiality (rūpa) has the characteristic of being molested (ruppana); feeling has the characteristic of being felt,” is called full- understanding as the known.

rows.pali: "Rūpaṃ aniccaṃ, vedanā aniccā"tiādinā nayena tesaṃyeva dhammānaṃ sāmaññalakkhaṇaṃ āropetvā pavattā lakkhaṇārammaṇikavipassanā paññā tīraṇapariññā nāma.
rows.english: The understanding consisting in insight with the general characteristics as its object that occurs in attributing a general characteristic to those same states in the way beginning, “Materiality is impermanent, feeling is impermanent” is called full-understanding as investigation.2

rows.pali: Tesuyeva pana dhammesu niccasaññādipajahanavasena pavattā lakkhaṇārammaṇikavipassanā paññā pahānapariññā nāma.
rows.english: The understanding consisting in insight with the characteristics as its object that occurs as the abandoning of the perception of permanence, etc., in those same states is called full-understanding as abandoning.

rows.pali: Tattha saṅkhāraparicchedato paṭṭhāya yāva paccayapariggahā ñātapariññāya bhūmi.
rows.english: 4.Herein, the plane of full-understanding as the known extends from the delimitation of formations (Ch. XVIII) up to the discernment of conditions (Ch. XIX);

rows.pali: Etasmiṃ hi antare dhammānaṃ paccattalakkhaṇapaṭivedhasseva ādhipaccaṃ hoti.
rows.english: for in this interval the penetration of the specific characteristics of states predominates.

rows.pali: Kalāpasammasanato pana paṭṭhāya yāva udayabbayānupassanā tīraṇapariññāya bhūmi.
rows.english: The plane of full-understanding as investigation extends from comprehension by groups up to contemplation of rise and fall (XXI.3f.);

rows.pali: Etasmiṃ hi antare sāmaññalakkhaṇapaṭivedhasseva ādhipaccaṃ hoti.
rows.english: for in this interval the penetration of the general characteristics predominates.

rows.pali: Bhaṅgānupassanaṃ ādiṃ katvā upari pahānapariññāya bhūmi.
rows.english: The plane of full-understanding as abandoning extends from contemplation of dissolution onwards (XXI.10);

rows.pali: Tato paṭṭhāya hi aniccato anupassanto niccasaññaṃ pajahati, dukkhato anupassanto sukhasaññaṃ, anattato anupassanto attasaññaṃ, nibbindanto nandiṃ, virajjanto rāgaṃ, nirodhento samudayaṃ, paṭinissajjanto ādānaṃ pajahatīti (paṭi. ma. 1.52) evaṃ niccasaññādipahānasādhikānaṃ sattannaṃ anupassanānaṃ ādhipaccaṃ.
rows.english: for from there onwards the seven contemplations that effect the abandoning of the perception of permanence, etc., predominate thus: (1) Contemplating [formations] as impermanent, a man abandons the perception of permanence. (2) Contemplating [them] as painful, he abandons the perception of pleasure. (3) Contemplating [them] as not-self, he abandons the perception of self. (4) Becoming dispassionate, he abandons delighting. (5) Causing fading away, he abandons greed. (6) Causing cessation, he abandons originating. (7) Relinquishing, he abandons grasping (Paṭis I 58).3

rows.pali: Iti imāsu tīsu pariññāsu saṅkhāraparicchedassa ceva paccayapariggahassa ca sādhitattā iminā yoginā ñātapariññāva adhigatā hoti, itarā ca adhigantabbā.
rows.english: 5. So, of these three kinds of full-understanding, only full-understanding as the known has been attained by this meditator as yet, which is because the delimitation of formations and the discernment of conditions have already been accomplished; the other two still remain to be attained.

rows.pali: Tena vuttaṃ "yasmā tīraṇapariññāya vattamānāya maggāmaggañāṇaṃ uppajjati, tīraṇapariññā ca ñātapariññānantarā, tasmāpi taṃ maggāmaggañāṇadassanavisuddhiṃ sampādetukāmena kalāpasammasane tāva yogo kātabbo"ti.
rows.english: Hence it was said above: “Besides, knowledge of what is the path and what is not the path arises when full-understanding as investigation is occurring, and full-understanding as investigation comes next to full-understanding as the known. So this is also a reason why one who desires to accomplish this purification by knowledge and vision of what is the path and what is not the path should first of all apply himself to comprehension by groups” (§2).

rows.pali: 694.Tatrāyaṃ pāḷi –
rows.english: 6.Here is the text:

rows.pali: Ettha ca cakkhuṃ - pe - jarāmaraṇanti iminā peyyālena dvārārammaṇehi saddhiṃ dvārappavattā dhammā, pañcakkhandhā, cha dvārāni, cha ārammaṇāni, cha viññāṇāni, cha phassā, cha vedanā, cha saññā, cha cetanā, cha taṇhā, cha vitakkā, cha vicārā, cha dhātuyo, dasa kasiṇāni, dvattiṃsakoṭṭhāsā, dvādasāyatanāni, aṭṭhārasa dhātuyo, bāvīsati indriyāni, tisso dhātuyo, nava bhavā, cattāri jhānāni, catasso appamaññā, catasso samāpattiyo, dvādasa paṭiccasamuppādaṅgānīti ime dhammarāsayo saṃkhittāti veditabbā.
rows.english: 9.Herein, the abbreviation, “The eye … (etc.) … Ageing-and-death,” should be understood to represent the following sets of things elided: 1. The states that occur in the doors [of consciousness] together with the doors and the objects. 2. The five aggregates. 3. The six doors. 4. The six objects. 5. The six kinds of consciousness. 6. The six kinds of contact. 7. The six kinds of feeling. 8. The six kinds of perception. 9. The six kinds of volition. 10. The six kinds of craving. 11. The six kinds of applied thought. 12. The six kinds of sustained thought. 13. The six elements. 14. The ten kasiṇas. 15. The thirty-two bodily aspects. 16. The twelve bases. 17. The eighteen elements. 18. The twenty-two faculties. 19. The three elements. 20. The nine kinds of becoming. 21. The four jhānas. 22. The four measureless states. 23. The four [immaterial] attainments. 24. The twelve members of the dependent origination.

rows.pali: Vuttaṃ hetaṃ abhiññeyyaniddese –
rows.english: 10. For this is said in the Paṭisambhidā in the description of what is to be directly known:

rows.pali: Taṃ tattha evaṃ vitthārena vuttattā idha sabbaṃ peyyālena saṃkhittaṃ.
rows.english: 12.Since all this detail is given there it has been abbreviated here.

rows.pali: Evaṃ saṃkhitte panettha ye lokuttarā dhammā āgatā, te asammasanupagattā imasmiṃ adhikāre na gahetabbā.
rows.english: But what is thus abbreviated includes the supramundane states. These should not be dealt with at this stage because they are not amenable to comprehension.

rows.pali: Yepi ca sammasanupagā, tesu ye yassa pākaṭā honti sukhena pariggahaṃ gacchanti, tesu tena sammasanaṃ ārabhitabbaṃ.
rows.english: And as regards those that are amenable to comprehension a beginning should be made by comprehending those among them that are obvious to and easily discernible by the individual [meditator].

rows.pali: 695.Tatrāyaṃ khandhavasena ārabbhavidhānayojanā – yaṃkiñci rūpaṃ - pe - sabbaṃ rūpaṃ aniccato vavatthapeti, ekaṃ sammasanaṃ.
